entries.author: Phyllis Cha

entries.title: Cook County state's attorney's office training new prosecutor task force to focus on CTA crimes
entries.summary: <p>The Cook County state's attorneys office is training some of its staff to bolster prosecutions of crime on the CTA.</p><p>A new internal transit crime task force will train more than 30 prosecutors and provide guidance on seeking court orders to restrict defendants from accessing parts of the CTA, according to State's Attorney Eileen O'Neill Burke's office.</p><p>The prosecutors will receive enhanced training on CTA video evidence, serve as liaisons with Chicago police and CTA on transit-related cases, and track data on transit crimes.</p><p>As part of the training, the assistant state's attorneys will visit the Chicago Police Department's Strategic Decision Support Centers to better understand the city's surveillance network. </p><p>Burke also issued an internal directive Wednesday calling for prosecutors to request restrictions on felony defendants from accessing specific CTA locations in cases when pretrial detention is denied or not an option. The directive also gives prosecutors discretion to request location restrictions in misdemeanor cases, but instructs prosecutors not to ban any defendant from the entire CTA system.</p><p>Burke's office says it is aware of the uptick in crime on transit, and is making prosecuting these crimes a priority.</p><p>"The goal of this task force is to make sure that we are effectively prosecuting these cases," says Anastasia Harper, deputy chief assistant state's attorney for policy and external affairs.</p><p>The idea is that this group of attorneys will pass on their knowledge from the training, such as contact info or prosecution strategies, to other members of the office, she said.</p><p>Training began earlier this year and is expected to continue through mid-April, she said.</p><p>Burke's initiative comes as the Trump administration <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/03/19/rta-nita-board-security-survey-cta-metra-pace" target="_blank" >backs off its $50 million funding threat</a> to the CTA over its stubbornly high crime rate.</p><p>Local elected officials have accused the U.S. Department of Transportation of unfairly picking on Chicago and other Democratic-run cities. But <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/03/02/chicago-cta-transit-crime-assault-battery-leerhsen" target="_blank" >reporting</a> from the Sun-Times shows violent attacks on the CTA are the highest they have been in a quarter century. The Chicago Tribune <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagotribune.com/2026/03/22/cta-rider-worker-assaults/" target="_blank" >reported</a> Sunday that the assault rate on the CTA is double that of peer agencies.</p><p>The Federal Transit Administration compelled the CTA to revise its safety plan earlier in March after rejecting the CTA's December plan to boost CPD's volunteer overtime transit patrol program. On March 10, the CTA said it was boosting police office presence on transit by 75%, with the help of sheriff's deputies who are now patrolling trains. The plan also hinted at collaboration with the state's attorneys office, but did not provide much detail. </p><p>The plan also calls for CPD to create a unified team of detectives covering only transit crimes within the next two months, according to a <a class="Link" href="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/96/16/407af3ff435a94d001699a6360d1/cta-revised-security-enhancement-plan-3-10-2026.pdf" target="_blank" >copy of the plan</a> provided to the Sun-Times through an open-records request.</p><p>Separately, the Department of Transportation has frozen $3 billion in federal grants for CTA projects, including the Red Line extension to 130th Street, while the Department says it analyzes the CTA's contractor hiring practices. The CTA last week <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/03/20/cta-red-line-extension-lawsuit" target="_blank" >sued the Department of Transportation</a> over the funding freeze, saying that work on the Red Line extension may soon halt if they money stays frozen.<br></p>
entries.author: David Struett

entries.title: Cubs over Brewers in division? National League Central's back-to-back-to-back champs aren't worried
entries.summary: <p>MILWAUKEE — So we’re doing this again, are we?</p><p>Talking about the big-market <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" target="_blank" >Cubs</a> as though the National League Central <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/03/23/cubs-have-a-series-commitment-in-2026-counsell-swanson-bregman" target="_blank" >is theirs to lose</a>?</p><p>Minimizing the mini-market Brewers as though maybe, if all goes right, they’ll little-engine-that-could their way back into the postseason picture?</p><p>All the major league previews in the national media, and all the sports gambling sites and apps in the degenerate-o-sphere, are indicating the Cubs should be expected to win in the neighborhood of 90 games.</p><p>And they’re telling you the Brewers — the back-to-back-to-back division champs, lest anyone forget — are more of a low-to-mid-80s type of outfit.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/03/23/cubs-have-a-series-commitment-in-2026-counsell-swanson-bregman" >Cubs have a (World) Series commitment in 2026</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/03/22/cubs-roster-president-jed-hoyer-seiya-suzuki-dylan-carlson-kevin-alcantara-michael-conforto-chas-mccormick-scott-kingery-nico-hoerner-craig-counsell-porter-hodge-justin-steele-ben-brown-javier-assad" >Cubs’ roster still has ‘balls in the air,’ Jed Hoyer says</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/03/20/cubs-2016-world-series-champions-matt-shaw-nico-hoerner-matthew-boyd-anthony-rizzo-porter-hodge" target="_blank" >Ten years later, Cubs would love to duplicate championship success</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>You want to believe that? Go ahead.</p><p>“I think a lot of people have been really waiting for the day that we suck and they can finally say, ‘We told you so, they’re no good,’ ” veteran outfielder Christian Yelich told the Sun-Times this week at American Family Field, where the Brewers tied a bow on their spring with two exhibition games before welcoming the White Sox for Opening Day.</p><p>“We’ve become accustomed to winning and being a postseason team, being a team that’s competing for the division. That’s definitely not changing this year.”</p><p>Seriously, why would it?</p><p>In 2023, win projections for the Brewers were right around 84½. On planet reality, though, they went 92-70. That was manager Craig Counsell’s last season before being lured away by the Cubs.</p><p>In 2024, with Counsell gone, ace Corbin Burnes gone and standout starting pitcher Brandon Woodruff in doubt for the season, the Brewers were projected to go under .500 and finish fourth in the division. Instead, they fared even better — 93 wins this time — and won the division by a whopping 10 games over the Cubs and Cardinals.</p><p>In 2025, it was the same old dance. The Brewers were projected to win around 83½ — and the Cubs, for the second year in a row, to take the division — but the Cubs finished looking up at a 97-win Brewers squad with the best record in the NL. And we know how the rivals’ division series matchup went down.</p><p>The Brewers have Pat Murphy, who has won back-to-back NL manager of the year awards. If Hall of Fame-bound Reds manager Terry Francona is the division’s best — and he is — that must make Counsell an unofficial third in the rankings.</p><p>They have president of baseball operations Matt Arnold, winner of back-to-back executive of the year awards. It seems Arnold has more ideas about what can be done with a dollar than Jed Hoyer and the rest of his peers can even imagine.</p><p>They have mostly the same lineup that knocked the Cubs out of the playoffs, with Jackson Chourio, Brice Turang, William Contreras and Yelich back for more; rock-solid base running and defense, which never goes out of style; and, again, an imposing bullpen featuring Abner Uribe and Trevor Megill.</p><p>Ace Freddy Peralta was traded, bolstering a farm system commonly ranked at the top of baseball. But Woodruff is back in the rotation — though he ramped up slowly and cautiously this spring — and fireballer Jacob Misiorowski, an instant superstar in 2025, is in an extremely rapid ascent.</p><p>There’s no one here expecting to take a step back.</p><p>“We know what this locker room can do,” Turang said. “That’s all that matters.”</p><p>Third baseman and utility player Luis Rengifo signed a modest free-agent deal in the offseason and has a chance to become the latest big-league veteran to find a higher level of performance and purpose in a Brewers uniform. Rengifo, who came from the Angels, has noted a hunger level in the clubhouse that’s greater than what he’d been exposed to and says his own desire to contribute anything he can to the cause of winning has been elevated by the guys around him.</p><p>“The things that are important here are all [about] winning games, always,” he said. “It’s just different here.”</p><p>Said outfielder Sal Frelick, “You’ve got to be a ballplayer to play here.”</p><p>Meaning what, exactly?</p><p>“Meaning drop a bunt down when needed, be a good base runner, play the best defense you can,” Frelick said. “Those three things — situational hitting, base running and defense — are who we are, and that means every one of us, and I know that’s not the case with every team. But it’s why I always believe in our team, always have the utmost belief in us.”</p><p>Asked about the projected win totals out there, Murphy made a joking show of tearing into the Brewers’ communications staff for not furnishing him with printed examples of the specifics.</p><p>“I keep asking for them,” he said. “I want evidence. Receipts. You know what I’m saying.”</p><p>But this is one confident skipper, as always.</p><p>“If someone tells me I suck, it usually challenges me to try to prove I don’t,” Murphy said.</p><p>“Buckle up and get ready, because it’s going to be a fight. There’ll be a lot of bloodshed and a lot of pain to get where you want to go.”</p><p>That goes for everybody in the Central, the favored Cubs included.</p><p>“I meant that figuratively,” Murphy said with a smile and a wink.</p><p>Thank goodness for that.</p>
entries.author: Steve Greenberg

entries.title: Semi, car crash leaves slabs of sheet metal on Kennedy, 'extended delays' expected on Northwest Side
entries.summary: <p>Scraps of sheet metal that slid off a semi truck during a crash with a car has caused the closure of multiple lanes on the Kennedy Expressway Wednesday morning on the Northwest Side. </p><p>Around 6:50 a.m., state troopers responded to a two-vehicle crash at Kostner Avenue near the split with the Edens Expressway involving a semi, Illinois state police said. </p><p>One person was taken to an area hospital where their condition wasn’t immediately known, state police said. </p><p>The left three lanes on the Kennedy were blocked by the crash and the right three lanes on the Edens Expressway side — which is nearby — are currently blocked by larges pieces of sheet metal, officials said. </p><p>Extended delays are expected and motorists are urged to seek an alternate route.<br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Jury finds Instagram and YouTube liable in landmark social media addiction trial
entries.summary: <p>LOS ANGELES (AP) — A jury found both Meta and YouTube liable in a <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/meta-instagram-facebook-trial-social-media-addiction-0e99c9ba6159421720d616f9facd10f0" target="_blank" >first-of-its-kind lawsuit</a> that aimed to hold social media platforms responsible for harm to children using their services, awarding the plaintiff $3 million in damages.</p><p>After more than 40 hours of deliberation across nine days, California jurors decided Meta and YouTube were negligent in the design or operation of their platforms. The jury also decided each company’s negligence was a substantial factor in causing harm to the <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/meta-instagram-facebook-trial-social-media-addiction-2afb4809d2dbbb0d1e69739c7f2b20b3" target="_blank" >plaintiff, a 20-year-old woman</a> who says her use of social media as a child addicted her to the technology and exacerbated her mental health struggles.</p><p>The multimillion-dollar verdict will grow, as the jury decided the companies acted with malice, or highly egregious conduct, meaning they will hear new evidence shortly and head back into the deliberation room to decide on punitive damages.</p><p>Meta and Google-owned YouTube were the two remaining defendants in the case after <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/social-media-trial-kids-addiction-meta-tiktok-youtube-d3a6bf617f2d11521675412ffb275031" target="_blank" >TikTok and Snap each settled</a> before the trial began.</p><p>“We respectfully disagree with the verdict and are evaluating our legal options,” Meta said in a statement.</p><p>Jurors listened to about a month of lawyers’ arguments, testimony and evidence, and they heard from the plaintiff herself, a 20-year-old woman identified as KGM in documents, or Kaley as her lawyers have called her during the trial, as well as Meta leaders <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/mark-zuckerberg-trial-testimony-instagram-c8cbaa32ccbf4933ec3a7beebd6cf34b" target="_blank" >Mark Zuckerberg</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/instagram-social-media-addiction-trial-mosseri-los-angeles-a49f64e21682e8bc1b98fbdf3d061579" target="_blank" >Adam Mosseri</a>. YouTube’s CEO, Neal Mohan, was not called in to testify.</p><p>Kaley says she began using YouTube at age 6 and Instagram at age 9 and told the jury she was <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/meta-instagram-facebook-trial-social-media-addiction-2afb4809d2dbbb0d1e69739c7f2b20b3" target="_blank" >on social media “all day long”</a> as a child.</p><p>Lawyers representing Kaley, led by Mark Lanier, were tasked with proving that the respective defendants’ negligence was a substantial factor in causing Kaley’s harm. They pointed to specific design features they said were designed to “hook” young users, like the “infinite” nature of feeds that allowed for an endless supply of content, autoplay features, and even notifications.</p><p>The jurors were told not to take into account the content of the posts and videos that Kaley saw on the platforms. That’s because tech companies are shielded from legal responsibility for content posted on their sites thanks to <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/us-supreme-court-technology-social-media-business-internet-eb89baf1fa30e245c030992b48a8a0ff" target="_blank" >Section 230</a> of the 1996 Communications Decency Act.</p><p>Meta consistently argued that Kaley had struggled with her mental health separate from her social media use, often pointing to her turbulent home life. Meta also said “not one of her therapists identified social media as the cause” of her mental health issues in a statement following closing arguments. But the plaintiffs did not have to prove that social media caused Kaley’s struggles — only that it was a “substantial factor” in causing her harm.</p><p>YouTube focused less on Kaley’s medical records and mental health history and more on her use of YouTube and the nature of the platform. They argued that YouTube is not a form of social media, but rather a video platform akin to television, and pointed to her declining YouTube use as she got older. According to their data, she spent about one minute a day on average watching YouTube Shorts since its inception. YouTube Shorts, which launched in 2020, is the platform’s section of short-form, vertical videos that have the “infinite scroll” feature the plaintiffs argued was addictive.</p><p>Lawyers representing both platforms also consistently pointed to the safety features and guardrails they each have available for people to monitor and customize their use.</p><p>The case, along with several others, has been randomly selected as a bellwether trial, meaning its outcome could impact how thousands of similar lawsuits filed against social media companies play out.</p><p>Laura Marquez-Garrett, an attorney with the Social Media Victims Law Center and the counsel of record for Kaley, said this trial was “a vehicle, not an outcome” during deliberations.</p><p>“This case is historic no matter what happens because it was the first,” Marquez-Garrett said, emphasizing the gravity of getting Meta and Google’s internal documents into the public record.</p><p>Marquez-Garrett said social media companies are “not taking the cancerous talcum powder off the shelves,” likely in reference to a past case that Lanier and his firm worked on, securing a multi-billion-dollar verdict. “And they’re not going to because they’re making too much money killing kids.”</p><p>Still, the Social Media Victims Law Center and the parents who trace their children’s deaths or harms back to social media will continue to keep fighting, Marquez-Garrett said, wearing several rubber wristbands in honor of victims that have not come off since the trial began.</p><p>The trial was one of several that <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/social-media-trials-meta-zuckerberg-youtube-tiktok-addiction-85c4d813c42845aeb3f913ec8f2f3e86" target="_blank" >social media companies face</a> this year and beyond. They are the culmination of years of scrutiny of the platforms over child safety, and whether the companies make them addictive and serve up content that leads to depression, eating disorders or suicide.</p><p>Some experts see the reckoning as reminiscent of cases against tobacco and <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/hub/opioids" target="_blank" >opioid markets</a>, and the plaintiffs hope that social media platforms will see similar outcomes as cigarette makers and drug companies, pharmacies and distributors.</p>
entries.author: Barbara Ortutay | AP

entries.title: Where to find Chicago's favorite craft beers
entries.summary: <p>Several beloved breweries across the Chicago area <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/food-drink/2026/03/11/chicago-brewery-closures-craft-beer-alarmist-illuminated-whiner-flapjack-casa-humilde" target="_blank" ><u>have shut off their taps</u></a> or announced that they’ll be doing so soon. The announcements have surviving brewers on guard, and a few said they are looking at everything from better-than-average food to live events to stay afloat.</p><p>But Chicago is still a beer town. And while a steady decline in drinking is partially to blame for our current state (the U.S. drinking rate reached historic lows in 2025, according to <a class="Link" href="https://news.gallup.com/poll/693362/drinking-rate-new-low-alcohol-concerns-surge.aspx" target="_blank" ><u>data from Gallup</u></a>), there are plenty of options to choose from and lots of variety.</p><p>Last week, my colleague <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/author/sofie-hernandez-simeonidis" target="_blank" >Sofie Hernandez-Simeonidis</a> asked WBEZ <a class="Link" href="https://joinsubtext.com/c/wbez" target="_blank" ><u>Subtext subscribers</u></a> to share their favorite local brews with us. We also posed the question on the Chicago Sun-Times <a class="Link" href="https://www.facebook.com/thechicagosuntimes/posts/pfbid023ab5YM7SjJJzSkAqyyhYdQzTCTD3YUHJf2vQiSo6XAohamGZntM6CnSPWAQEMG1zl" target="_blank" >Facebook page</a>.</p><h3></h3><h3>Here’s what you had to say.</h3><h3></h3><h3>Chicago</h3><p>“I LOVE the beers at<b> Industry Ales,</b> downtown in the Loop next to Central Cameras, “ wrote a Subtext subscriber. <a class="Link" href="https://www.industryales.com/" target="_blank" ><i><u>Industry Ales</u></i></a><i>, 230 S. Wabash Ave.; open Weds. and Thurs.</i></p><p>“<b>Hop Butcher</b> is my favorite — you can’t go wrong with anything by them but their hazys are something special,” wrote a Subtext subscriber. <a class="Link" href="http://www.hopbutcher.com/" target="_blank" ><i><u>Hop Butcher For The World</u></i></a><i>, 4257 N. Lincoln Ave.; closed Mondays</i></p><p>“I’m a <b>Begyle </b>kinda girl,” wrote Analia Beltran-Lang. <a class="Link" href="http://www.begylebrewing.com/" target="_blank" ><i><u>Begyle Brewing</u></i></a><i>, 1800 W. Cuyler Ave.; open daily</i></p><p>“<b>Suncatcher</b> is a really cool new brewery,” wrote a Subtext subscriber. <a class="Link" href="https://suncatcherbrewing.com/" target="_blank" ><i><u>Suncatcher Brewing</u></i></a><i>,2849 W. Chicago Ave.; closed Tuesdays</i></p><p>“<b>Old Irving Brewing Co.</b>[’s] Beezer is my absolute favorite hazy IPA, but it is just the beginning at OIB!” wrote Shannan Hofman Bunting. <a class="Link" href="https://oldirvingbrewing.com/" target="_blank" ><i><u>Old Irving Brewing Co.</u></i></a><i>, 4419 W. entries.author: Ambar Colón

entries.title: Human Relations Commissioner Andrade resigns after concerns antisemitism report draft was 'whitewashed'
entries.summary: <p>Chicago’s human relations commissioner has resigned to protest what critics contend was an attempt by Mayor Brandon Johnson’s administration to whitewash a long-awaited report that was supposed to focus solely on antisemitism.</p><p>Johnson’s relationship with many Jewish leaders has been strained ever since he cast the tie-breaking vote in favor of a non-binding resolution demanding a cease-fire in the Israel-Hamas conflict. Nancy Andrade’s resignation to protest the attempted whitewash could bring the situation to a boil.</p><p>Human Relations Commission member Dan Goldwin said the report triggered by a 58% rise in reported anti-Jewish hate crimes from 2023 to 2024 was completed and focused solely on the surge in incidents of antisemitism in Chicago — and what to do about it.</p><p>But when the report was forwarded to the mayor’s office in November, a consultant was hired to alter the report and turn it into a broader document on all hate incidents, Goldwin said. </p><p>“The mayor’s office sent back an edit of it that completely whitewashed it… They had just crossed off anything that had to do with anti-Jewish hate crime and just made it an all-lives matter” report, said Goldwin, who also serves as chief public affairs officer for the Jewish United Fund.</p><p>“The commission said no… We wouldn’t do it. That’s why we voted as a commission in early February to send our original draft back to the mayor.”</p><p>Goldwin described Andrade as the “loudest and clearest voice that was standing up to what was happening to the Jewish community in the city of Chicago.”</p><p>“It’s a real loss that she’s gone,” Goldwin said.</p><p>Johnson's communications director Erin Connelly denied any attempt by the mayor's office to whitewash the report.</p><p>Andrade did not return repeated phone calls. Her resignation letter does not make reference to the original report on antisemitic hate or the changes that the mayor’s office tried to make.</p><p>It simply describes the role that the Human Relations Commission is supposed to play as a “beacon of light that gives hope to all Chicagoans who have experienced the darkness of hate,” and that offers “justice to all who have experienced the humiliation and trauma of discrimination.” </p><p>“As the head of the CCHR, but also as a licensed attorney, I took an oath to uphold these values, as well as a strict code of ethics. My resignation is in service of that oath,” Andrade, a holdover from former Mayor Lori Lightfoot’s administration, said in the letter. </p><p>“The CCHR is the civil rights agency for ALL of Chicago. It is my sincere hope that the CCHR and its Board continue to focus on addressing the impact of antisemitism as well as all other forms of discrimination in our city,” she wrote.</p><p>Ald. Debra Silverstein (50th), the City Council’s lone Jewish member, was among the Jewish community leaders who showed up in force at the Feb. 11 Human Relations Commission meeting demanding that the commission approve the unedited version of the report, which focused solely on the surge in antisemitic hate in Chicago.</p><p>“They did. They voted on the original version — not the mayor’s version,” she said.</p><p>“That’s the report that was sent to the mayor’s office and is still currently sitting on his desk. He’s done nothing about implementing any of the recommendations about setting up a task force, about education” and other changes proposed to protect Jewish Chicagoans.</p><p>Silverstein said the Jewish community has “not felt protected” by the Johnson administration “for a while now,” and Andrade’s resignation will exacerbate those tensions.</p><p>“We actually feel very alienated and the mayor has done nothing over the last several years to make us feel protected. This is just one more thing that is showing us that the mayor doesn’t have our back,” Silverstein said. “Nancy has been a very strong ally to the Jewish community and I’m concerned about her replacement and how that’s going to affect the Jewish community going forward.”</p><p>Johnson got off on the wrong foot with Jewish leaders by casting the tie-breaking vote on the cease-fire resolution. The relationship was further strained by Johnson’s refusal to fire his chief lobbyist Kennedy Bartley and remove Ald. Rossana Rodriguez-Sanchez (33rd) as chair of the City Council’s Committee on Health and Human Relations for their social media posts viewed by Jewish leaders as antisemitic.</p><p>The mayor initially defended his appointment of Rev. Mitchell Johnson as president of the Chicago Board of Education, only to have Johnson resign the following day after antisemitic comments were found on his social media pages.</p><p>Yet another chapter was written last year when Johnson refused a demand made by a City Council majority to condemn as antisemitic a piece of artwork on display at the Chicago Cultural Center titled “U.S.-Israel War Machine.” The Council majority wanted the artwork removed.</p><p>It featured a puppet branded “Uncle Sam” with blood on his face wearing a t-shirt that includes a tank, the word “money,” a dollar sign and the words, “Thank You” with a smiley face. Standing next to the Uncle Sam figure was a puppet of Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu with blood on his hands.</p><p>Next to the second puppet’s hand was an MK-84 aircraft bomb on a stick, with smoke coming out of the explosive device. The artwork also featureed a bucket labeled “TEARS” with four oversized drops coming out of it. Wooden posts supporting the puppets include the words, “child killer.”</p><p>"I’ve seen very provocative artwork that depicts slavery. I’ve seen artwork where a noose with the colors of the American flag were gripped around a Black man. Very provocative,” Johnson said then.</p><p>“It’s important — particularly at a time when history and culture is being threatened and undermined — that we don’t find ourselves exacerbating the attempt to silence the voices of individuals that speak their truth through their lived experiences... To have any discourse shut down is something that we should be very wary of.”</p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: White Sox set Opening Day roster
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a> finalized their Opening Day roster Wednesday ahead of their final workout in Milwaukee before kicking off their season Thursday at American Family Field.</p><p>Days after the surprise acquisition of catcher <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/22/white-sox-sign-catcher-reese-mcguire-leaving-korey-lee-as-odd-man-out" >Reese McGuire</a>, the team designated backstop Korey Lee for assignment along with infielder Curtis Mead, who played for <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/15/white-sox-curtis-mead-encouraged-after-eventful-spring-with-roster-cuts-looming" target="_blank" ><u>Australia in the World Baseball Classic</u></a>.</p><p>With promising second-year catcher Kyle Teel starting the year on the 10-day injured list with a hamstring injury from the WBC, the Sox will open the season with two catchers on the 26-man roster, McGuire and Edgar Quero.</p><p>The rotation to start the year is Shane Smith, Sean Burke, Anthony Kay, Davis Martin and Erick Fedde.</p><p>The bullpen includes left-handers Tyler Gilbert, Chris Murphy, Sean Newcomb, along with righties Seranthony Domínguez, Jordan Hicks, Jordan Leasure, Jedixson Páez and Grant Taylor.</p><p>They’ll carry five infielders: Chase Meidroth, Colson Montgomery, Munetaka Murakami, Lenyn Sosa and Miguel Vargas.</p><p>And they’ll start the year with six outfielders: Luisangel Acuña, Andrew Benintendi, Austin Hays, Derek Hill, Everson Pereira and Tristan Peters.</p><p>Utility plate Brooks Baldwin will start on the 10-day IL with an elbow sprain, while righties Prelander Berroa and Drew Thorpe start on the 60-day IL in their recoveries from Tommy John surgery, as does Mike Vasil, who blew out his elbow <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/17/white-sox-mike-vasil-to-undergo-season-ending-tommy-john-surgery" target="_blank" ><u>earlier this month</u></a> and will miss the entire season.</p><h3><b>Sox at Brewers</b></h3><p><b>Thursday</b>: Shane Smith (0-0, 0.00 ERA) vs. Jacob Misiorowski (0-0, 0.00), 1:10 p.m., CHSN, 1000-AM.</p><p><b>Saturday</b>: Sean Burke (0-0, 0.00) vs. Chad Patrick (0-0, 0.00), 6:10 p.m., CHSN, 1000-AM.</p><p><b>Sunday</b>: Anthony Kay (0-0, 0.00) vs. Brandon Sproat, 1:10 p.m., CHSN, 1000-AM.</p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Alcaldesa de Broadview exige cierre de instalación de ICE y el reembolso de pérdidas económicas del suburbio
entries.summary: <p>La alcaldesade Broadview, suburbio al oeste de Chicago, está pidiendo al gobierno federal que cierre un centro de detención de inmigrantes “de facto” que fue el epicentro de protestas —y respuestas violentas por parte de agentes federales— que envolvieron al pequeño suburbio el otoño pasado.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/immigration/2025/10/04/broadview-mayor-katrina-thompson-ice-detention-center-arrests-kristi-noem-gregory-bovino" target="_blank" >La alcaldesa Katrina Thompson</a> también está solicitando a las autoridades federales un reembolso de más de $700,000 para cubrir los costos operativos incurridos por la Municipalidad y las pérdidas económicas sufridas por dos negocios adyacentes a las instalaciones del Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) durante el Operativo Midway Blitz, la intensa campaña de deportación que alcanzó su punto máximo de septiembre a noviembre.</p><p>Thompson presentó las solicitudes durante una visita a Washington, D.C., a principios de este mes y en una carta dirigida a varios miembros de la delegación congresional de Illinois.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-2b0000" name="module-2b0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>“Broadview no pidió este operativo, y nuestros residentes, nuestros oficiales de policía, nuestros bomberos, nuestros dueños de negocios no deberían ser quienes paguen por ello”, dijo Thompson en un comunicado de prensa el martes. “Abogué por el reembolso federal para comunidades como la nuestra afectadas por operativos de ICE, enfatizando que los contribuyentes locales no deberían asumir el costo de responsabilidades federales. También insistí en el cierre del Centro de Procesamiento de ICE en Broadview, centrándonos en la dignidad, la seguridad y una visión más fuerte para nuestra comunidad”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c50000" name="image-c50000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source alt="La alcaldesa Katrina Thompson" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7678f9d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F14%2F41%2F0a58daff40b8aa7cbd03ec14eddf%2Fbroadviewmayor-100525-19.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b120a01/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F14%2F41%2F0a58daff40b8aa7cbd03ec14eddf%2Fbroadviewmayor-100525-19.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7678f9d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F14%2F41%2F0a58daff40b8aa7cbd03ec14eddf%2Fbroadviewmayor-100525-19.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>La alcaldesa Katrina Thompson busca dejar atrás los problemas que rodean al centro de ICE en el suburbio del oeste.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Archivo Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Los costos municipales asociados con la respuesta a la actividad cerca de la instalación de ICE ascendieron a $361,536, según funcionarios de Broadview.</p><p>Registros obtenidos por el Chicago Sun-Times a través de una solicitud de la ley de Libertad de Información (FOIA) muestran que $71,185 de esa suma se pagaron en horas extras para la policía, con más de $3,000 destinados a equipos de protección personal adicionales para los oficiales. El suburbio gastó aproximadamente $41,000 en costos legales, incluido un juicio federal para retirar una cerca erigida alrededor de la instalación de ICE. Las transferencias en ambulancia desde la dirección de la instalación de ICE costaron casi $250,000. Y las horas extras para el personal de ambulancias y bomberos costaron cerca de $12,100.</p><p>Incluso antes del reembolso federal solicitado, aún no estaba claro si el poblado recuperaría alguna parte de ese dinero, como los transportes de paramédicos que podrían ser pagados a través del seguro de Medicaid.</p><p>Los funcionarios de Broadview también señalaron pérdidas de $353,813 para dos negocios locales dentro de dos cuadras de la instalación de ICE: Reynolds Advanced Materials y Wagner Brass Foundry, Inc. Ninguno de los negocios hizo comentarios de inmediato el martes.</p><p>La agitación que sacudió el suburbio, que tiene aproximadamente 8,000 residentes, en otoño se trasladó a los jardines frontales y patios traseros de muchos vecinos. Cientos de personas se presentaron casi a diario para protestar frente a la instalación donde la gran mayoría de los inmigrantes arrestados durante el Operativo Midway Blitz fueron llevados para su procesamiento antes de ser trasladados a centros de detención en el Medio Oeste.</p><p>Las condiciones descritas por los detenidos y los defensores como “inhumanas” se agravaron en el edificio sobrepoblado, donde las personas dormían en el suelo con acceso limitado a comida o agua.</p><p>Los manifestantes, incluidos miembros del clero, fueron recibidos con la fuerza de agentes federales de ICE y de la Protección de Aduanas y Fronteras (CBP) que a menudo perseguían a los manifestantes por los vecindarios y disparaban bolas de goma y gas lacrimógeno contra las multitudes.</p><p>En su carta a los legisladores, Thompson solicitó ayuda para cerrar la instalación y transferir su propiedad del gobierno federal al suburbio para desarrollarlo como un museo. El “Broadview Justice Corridor”, como llamó al proyecto, convertiría la instalación de ICE en un “centro nacional dedicado a la historia de la inmigración, la educación en derechos civiles, el liderazgo cívico y el turismo cultural”.</p><p>“Esta transformación convertiría un sitio asociado con el miedo, las protestas y la división en uno que promueve la educación, la reconciliación y el diálogo nacional”, escribió Thompson en la carta, estimando que la propuesta podría atraer hasta 250,000 visitantes anuales y generar hasta $31 millones en actividad económica.</p><p>Un portavoz de Broadview dijo que Thompson aún no ha recibido respuesta a su apelación a los dos senadores y seis representantes, todos demócratas.</p><p>Pero es poco probable que influya en la administración de línea dura del presidente Donald Trump, que ha buscado abrir nuevos centros de detención de inmigrantes en el último año en lugar de cerrar cualquier instalación de ICE. Algunos de los legisladores que recibieron la apelación de Thompson fueron criticados por funcionarios de la administración y se les negó la entrada a la instalación de Broadview el año pasado cuando intentaron inspeccionar las condiciones.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Lauren FitzPatrick

entries.title: NFL wants to use replay to help replacement refs in case of labor impasse
entries.summary: <p>If the NFL has to use replacement referees this season, it wants to be prepared.</p><p>That’s why one of the five playing rules proposals that will be considered at next week’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >NFL Annual Meetings</a> is for the league’s officiating department to be able to correct “clear and obvious mistakes” made in the moment with replay — but only if there’s a work stoppage between the league and the NFL Referees Association.</p><p>“We're in the make-sure-the-game-can-go on business if we're stuck in the position that we're currently heading towards … ” competition committee co-chairman Rich McKay said Wednesday. “We're going to have new officials that haven't officiated NFL games with a little bit of a different set of rules in certain areas. The game's going to be a fast game. We think they'll be able to do it very competently. We just were trying to go in what areas could we help assist them.”</p><p>The Collective Bargaining Agreement between the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/08/bears-transaction-tracker-2026-offseason-free-agency-coming-going-ryan-poles-ben-johnson" target="_blank" >NFL</a> and the NFLRA expires at the end of May.</p><p>The last time there was a work impasse between the league and officials was at the start of the 2012 season. Replay technology — and its ability to clarify on-field calls — has changed dramatically.</p><p>“Replay Assist already allows us to do things that we never could do in 2012 and review a ton of plays,” McKay said.</p><p>The four other rules proposals submitted by the competition committee include allowing teams to declare an onside kick at any point in the game; to eliminate the incentive to kick the ball out of bounds when teams kick off from the 50-yard line after a penalty; to change the alignment of kick return teams; and to allow league representatives to consult with officials during the game when considering whether to eject a player.</p><p>Two teams submitted resolutions. The Steelers want to make permanent the rule that allows teams to have up to five video calls with prospective free agents during the legal tampering period, while the Browns want teams to be permitted to trade draft picks five years into the future.</p><p><b>NOTE:</b> McKay said the competition committee did not discuss the NFL’s ruling that the Bears were not due compensatory picks after losing assistant GM Ian Cunningham to the Falcons. That’s under the purview of the Workplace Diversity Committee, he said, though he was unsure if they’d debate it at the annual meetings.</p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Delivery robot smashes into Old Town bus shelter in second crash of the week
entries.summary: <p>A delivery robot shattered the glass of an Old Town bus shelter Tuesday in the second such incident of its kind.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://x.com/rquinnmyers/status/2036614748251226282?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw%7Ctwcamp%5Etweetembed%7Ctwterm%5E2036614748251226282%7Ctwgr%5Ec70601c467e0eac1de0d5551ea11cf89ab6bcbfa%7Ctwcon%5Es1_&ref_url=https%3A%2F%2Fblockclubchicago.org%2F2026%2F03%2F25%2Frobots-gone-wild-food-delivery-robots-smash-2-bus-shelters-in-chicago%2F" target="_blank" >Video captured the moments</a> after the robot, belonging to Coco Robotics, damaged the bus shelter near the intersection of Larrabee Street and North Avenue. The shelter was repaired by Wednesday morning, but broken glass was still visible in the surrounding area.</p><p>Coco Robotics was aware of the incident and is launching an internal investigation. </p><p>"Across more than one million miles of deliveries, this is the first time one of our robots has collided with a structure like this. Our robots operate at a top speed of about 5 miles per hour, and safety is a top priority in how we design and monitor our systems," said Carl Hansen, Vice President and Head of Government Relations at Coco Robotics. "While this appears to be a rare, isolated event, we are committed to learning from it and ensuring it does not happen again."</p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A Coco delivery robot makes its way to a customer to deliver some food to a customer in the Near West Side.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><p>Earlier this week, video of a delivery robot capturing the aftermath of a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/24/delivery-robot-goes-viral-after-shattering-west-town-bus-shelter-glass" target="_blank" >similar incident in West Town</a> went viral and prompted responses from Ald. Walter Red Burnett (27th) and Serve Robotics, the owner of the robot.</p><p>"We are stressing the importance of public safety and consistent monitoring of these robots to prevent harm to people, infrastructure, and property,” Burnett said in a statement. “We have been informed that the company is in talks with the bus shelter provider, and both parties will look to have it repaired as soon as possible. We are glad that no one was hurt.”</p><p>Serve Robotics was aware of the incident and worked with the bus shelter provider to quickly clean up the damages. The company was also reviewing what happened in an attempt to make improvements.</p><p>“We have also been in contact with local stakeholders and are committed to addressing any concerns directly,” Serve Robotics said in a statement. “We take this matter very seriously.”</p><p>Serve and Coco are <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/business/2026/delivery-robots-chicago-food-coco-robotics" target="_blank" >operating bots on Chicago’s north and west sides</a> under a <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicago.gov/city/en/depts/mayor/press_room/press_releases/2022/september/PersonalDeliveryDevicePDDPilotProgramPasses.html" target="_blank" ><u>pilot program</u></a> the City Council passed in 2022. Coco came to town in 2024, and Serve started deploying its fleet last September.</p><p>A <a class="Link" href="https://nosidewalkbots.org/" target="_blank" ><u>petition</u></a> to pause the program until after a public hearing has garnered over 3,700 signatures and in the 27th Ward, Burnett says he’ll continue to take feedback on the program from residents and local businesses.</p><p>“We will consider their performance in complying with safety protocols before making a determination on their presence in the ward following the pilot,” Burnett said.<br></p>

entries.title: Cómo la guerra en Irán eleva más que los precios de gas en Chicago
entries.summary: <p>Los residentes de Chicago se sienten presionados por el aumento de los precios de la gasolina, tras el ataque de Estados Unidos e Israel a Irán el mes pasado. Y los expertos dicen que el dolor financiero para los consumidores por la guerra en Irán no se detendrá en la bomba de gasolina.</p><p>“Va a ser como una serpiente que se traga a un ratón”, dijo Rachel Bronson, investigadora principal sobre energía y geopolítica en el Chicago Council on Global Affairs. “Vas a ver cómo afecta a la economía global durante un período significativo de tiempo”.</p><p>Esto se debe a que Irán ha cerrado el Estrecho de Ormuz, la estrecha entrada del Golfo Pérsico, donde pasa el 20% del suministro de petróleo del mundo.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-420000" name="module-420000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Según Sam Ori, director ejecutivo del Instituto de Clima y Crecimiento Sostenible de la Universidad de Chicago, es la mayor interrupción que ha enfrentado la industria petrolera.</p><p>“Nunca ha habido nada en la historia del mercado del petróleo que realmente esté a esta escala. Estás hablando de un quinto de los suministros de petróleo del mundo siendo interrumpidos”, dijo Ori. “Hay algunas rutas alternativas, pero en el mejor de los casos, todavía estás hablando de una interrupción del suministro de 10 [millones] a 15 millones de barriles al día”.</p><p>Dijo que la economía de Estados Unidos es "muy diferente" de lo que era en la década de 1970, cuando hubo una crisis energética.</p><p>“Usamos aproximadamente un tercio de petróleo por dólar de [producto interno bruto] que lo que hacíamos en ese entonces”, dijo. “La economía de Estados Unidos es simplemente una economía mucho menos intensa en petróleo en comparación con lo que era”.</p><p>Aún así, cualquier tipo de interrupción en los mercados petroleros, incluso en el extranjero, impactará los precios en Estados Unidos.</p><p>“Lo verán en la bomba, obviamente, pero con el tiempo, [los consumidores] lo verán en todos los precios más altos, en cualquier bien manufacturado que requiera una entrada de energía. Lo verán en los costos de construcción, en todo lo que necesitemos construir”, dijo Bronson.</p><p>A medida que la guerra en Irán continúa, aquí está cómo el aumento del costo del petróleo podría impactar a los residentes de Chicago.</p><h3>Costos de combustible</h3><p>Hasta el martes, el precio promedio del combustible regular en Chicago es de $4.46 por galón, un aumento del 19% en comparación con el mismo tiempo el año pasado. También es más alto en comparación con el mismo tiempo del mes pasado, cuando el precio promedio era de $3.12 por galón, según GasBuddy.</p><p>A nivel nacional, el precio promedio del combustible regular es de $3.98, el más alto hasta ahora este año.</p><p>“Los precios de la gasolina son de lo que tú y yo nos vamos a quejar, pero al final del día, es el precio del diésel lo que tiene un impacto mucho mayor en la economía de Estados Unidos”, dijo Patrick De Haan, jefe de análisis de petróleo en GasBuddy. “Todo en esta economía se mueve con diésel”.</p><h3>Transporte de mercancías</h3><p>El setenta por ciento del petróleo consumido a diario en Estados Unidos está en el sector del transporte, según Ori. Un aumento en el combustible de transporte, como el diésel, actúa como un impuesto oculto sobre las entregas.</p><p>Andrew Krop, director financiero de Spot, dijo que las tarifas de combustible no han subido tanto desde 2022. La compañía de logística de terceros, que tiene una oficina en Chicago, sirve principalmente a la industria de alimentos y bebidas.</p><p>Los costos de combustible de Spot, que constituyen del 20% al 25% de los gastos operativos, ya han aumentado un 40% en comparación con el año pasado, dijo Krop.</p><p>La industria también ha estado lidiando con una escasez de conductoresn de camiones, lo que pone más presión sobre las empresas de transporte.</p><p>Dijo que sus clientes de camiones están "realmente preocupados" y anticipa que el aumento de los costos de combustible comenzará a reflejarse en el precio de los productos vendidos a los consumidores.</p><p>Michael Mahler, director de operaciones de HMD Trucking, con sede en Chicago Ridge, dijo que los precios en aumento del combustible son preocupantes.</p><p>"El aumento de los precios del combustible está teniendo un impacto considerable en las operaciones de nuestra empresa", dijo. "Aunque en este momento estamos experimentando un ligero ciclo ascendente de aproximadamente el 5% en las tarifas de flete hasta ahora este año, el precio por galón ha aumentado en un 30% o más durante el mismo período. Esta es una tendencia preocupante".</p><p>El transporte, la logística, el envío y la agricultura son alimentados por diésel, dijo De Haan, y esto podría llevar a más <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/economy/2026/03/18/fed-projects-one-rate-cut-this-year-powell-doj-investigation" target="_blank" >inflación</a> para consumidores que ya luchan con los gastos crecientes.</p><h3>Precios de los pasajes aéreos</h3><p>United Airlines, con sede en Chicago, envió la semana pasada una <a class="Link" href="https://www.united.com/en/us/newsroom/announcements/cision-125448?utm_source=newsletter&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=newsletter_axiosam&stream=top" target="_blank" >carta</a> al personal en la que anunció que reduciría su capacidad de vuelo en aproximadamente un 5%, citando el aumento en los precios del combustible para aviones. Cancelaría vuelos fuera de hora pico, como los vuelos nocturnos, durante el segundo y tercer trimestre de este año, con planes de estar a plena capacidad en otoño.</p><p>"La realidad es que, en las últimas tres semanas, los precios del combustible para aviones se han más que duplicado. Si los precios se mantienen en este nivel, eso significaría un gasto adicional de $11 [mil millones] anuales sólo por combustible para aviones. Para ponerlo en perspectiva, en el mejor año de United, ganamos menos de $5 [mil millones]", dijo el director ejecutivo de United, Scott Kirby, en su carta. "Eso puede sonar aterrador, pero la primera buena noticia es que, por ahora al menos, la demanda sigue siendo la más fuerte que hemos visto. Las 10 semanas de mayores ingresos reservados en nuestra historia han sido las últimas 10 semanas".</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d7e0fa" name="image-d7e0fa"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7353eda/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+100/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fef%2F28%2F94ebe0fb4b59bda7d3260dcd4e74%2Fairportice-260324-10.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/477364b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+100/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fef%2F28%2F94ebe0fb4b59bda7d3260dcd4e74%2Fairportice-260324-10.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/639b62a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+100/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fef%2F28%2F94ebe0fb4b59bda7d3260dcd4e74%2Fairportice-260324-10.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Los viajeros esperan en la fila en la Terminal 1 del Aeropuerto O’Hare el 23 de marzo de 2026." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/639b62a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+100/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fef%2F28%2F94ebe0fb4b59bda7d3260dcd4e74%2Fairportice-260324-10.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/605eb8d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+100/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fef%2F28%2F94ebe0fb4b59bda7d3260dcd4e74%2Fairportice-260324-10.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/639b62a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+100/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fef%2F28%2F94ebe0fb4b59bda7d3260dcd4e74%2Fairportice-260324-10.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Los viajeros esperan en la fila en la Terminal 1 del Aeropuerto O’Hare el 23 de marzo de 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Southwest Airlines, que recientemente <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/03/13/southwest-airlines-ohare" target="_blank" >eliminó su servicio</a> en el Aeropuerto O'Hare, se negó a comentar, diciendo que el impacto de los costos del combustible y su capacidad se discutiría cuando informe sus ingresos trimestrales el próximo mes.</p><p>Bronson dijo que las aerolíneas están absorbiendo los costos, pero pronto "tendrán que trasladar eso a los consumidores".</p><h3>Fertilizantes y alimentos</h3><p>Los precios de los comestibles en Chicago ya han aumentado en dígitos dobles durante el último año, pero los expertos dicen que la guerra en Irán podría llevar a aumentos adicionales de precios en la caja de pago.</p><p>Esto se debe a que el petróleo se utiliza comúnmente en la producción de fertilizantes. El Índice de Precios de Fertilizantes de Green Markets Weekly para América del Norte, que mide el precio de los fertilizantes, ponderado por la demanda anual de nutrientes para reflejar las tendencias del mercado, mostró un aumento del 31% desde el inicio de la guerra el 28 de febrero hasta el 24 de marzo.</p><p>“El impacto número uno serán los precios de los alimentos, debido al precio de los fertilizantes”, dijo Phillip Braun, profesor de la Universidad Northwestern. “Se verá afectado ya sea por el aumento en los costos de fertilizantes o por menores [cosechas] debido al costo de los fertilizantes”.</p><p>Bronson dijo que, además del aumento en los costos de fertilizantes, los productores verán que los costos aumentan en otras áreas también. Por ejemplo, el precio de una naranja producida fuera de Estados Unidos también costará más debido a costos de transporte más altos. Y eso conducirá a que los compradores paguen más por la naranja a la larga, o llevará a que las tiendas no tengan tantas naranjas o ninguna, dijo.</p><h3>Precios de medicamentos</h3><p>Braun dijo que el impacto de la guerra en los precios de los medicamentos probablemente será un "ardor lento" ya que la mayoría de las recetas se dispensan en cantidades de 30 a 60 días, y las farmacias a veces tienen suministros acumulados.</p><p>Y con la cobertura de seguros establecida para el año, los titulares de pólizas probablemente estarán a salvo —por ahora— de los aumentos en los costos de las recetas.</p><p>“[Los precios del petróleo] van a impactar a los [medicamentos] genéricos, especialmente porque tienen márgenes muy bajos... Impactará directamente a los compradores de genéricos”, dijo Braun.</p><p>Y algunos medicamentos, como el acetaminofén y los medicamentos para el colesterol, se fabrican a partir de petroquímicos que se derivan del petróleo y el gas natural.</p><p>“Puedes ver [el petróleo] apareciendo en todo... desde productos farmacéuticos hasta los plásticos que van en los envases de alimentos”, dijo Ori.</p><h3>Facturas de servicios públicos</h3><p>Si bien las facturas de servicios públicos han estado aumentando tras los aumentos de tarifas de compañías como <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/03/peoples-gas-hike-city-hall" target="_blank" >Peoples Gas</a>, los consumidores pueden estar tranquilos sabiendo que la crisis energética no debería contribuir a un aumento en las facturas de servicios públicos.</p><p>Ori dijo que el suministro de gas natural del país está "bastante aislado", a diferencia de lugares en Europa y Asia.</p><p>“Es una situación muy seria en partes de Asia”, dijo Ori. “Allí están pasando a semanas laborales de cuatro días. Están implementando prohibiciones en el uso del aire acondicionado”.</p><p>Si el Estrecho de Ormuz se abre pronto, Ori estima que podría tardar hasta varios meses antes de que los precios al consumidor comiencen a disminuir, tensionando una situación ya delicada para los estadounidenses que viven de cheque en cheque.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Mariah Rush

entries.title: Blackhawks need to find young forward who can take over Jason Dickinson's job
entries.summary: <p>PHILADELPHIA — For as encouraging overall as the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a>' win Tuesday over the Islanders was, the lopsided third period highlighted one new weakness in their lineup.</p><p>For years, the Hawks have direly lacked firepower in the top six but always had a reliable checking line to lean on, at least whenever Jason Dickinson was healthy.</p><p>During Dickinson's four years in Chicago, he emerged as legitimately one of the best defensive centers in the NHL, and Ilya Mikheyev emerged last season as an excellent right-hand man for him.</p><p>Now, Anton Frondell's arrival and Nick Lardis' growth — not to mention Connor Bedard and Frank Nazar's returns to full health — have juiced up the top six, which is starting to resemble its likely 2026-27 composition. Roman Kantserov's arrival next fall will inject even more offensive juice.</p><p>But at the same time, Dickinson and Nick Foligno's <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/04/blackhawks-oilers-trade-jason-dickinson-colton-dach-draft-pick" target="_blank" >trade</a> departures have decimated the defensive stoutness of the bottom six. Analytically, Mikheyev's even-strength defense has also taken a step back this season, even though he has thrived on the penalty kill. Teuvo Teravainen's play across the board has dipped, too.</p><p>When the Islanders pushed hard <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/24/blackhawks-islanders-anton-frondell-nhl-debut-nick-lardis" target="_blank" >Tuesday</a>, the Hawks discovered they didn't have a line they could rely on to possess the puck, tilt the ice, squash scoring chances and disrupt the home team's momentum — things Dickinson usually found a way to do.</p><p>The third line of Ryan Donato centering Mikheyev and Andre Burakovsky got caved in; chances favored the Islanders 8-1 during their five-on-five ice time. Bedard's line fared no better in best-on-best shifts.</p><p>Slotting Teravainen into Burakovsky's place might help a little bit, but there's simply no evidence Donato — whose defensive analytics have always been subpar, even during his career season — can be an effective checking center on his own.</p><p>During Donato's three-year Hawks tenure, he has been successful next to Dickinson (plus-five goal differential) and next to Bedard (plus-two), but he has struggled mightily apart from them (minus-30). His scoring-chance ratio with Dickinson was 51.9%; without him it's 41.7%.</p><p>So which Hawks forwards — specifically which young forwards — will step up as their defensive anchors moving forward? </p><p>That question seems to be on the front of coach Jeff Blashill's mind. He brought up Tuesday that "somebody, ultimately, is going to have to be a guy that learns to play against the other teams' best."</p><p>"Whoever that is, I don't know that yet," Blashill added. "You can sometimes use your top two lines for that, but that taxes them a lot. Most teams throughout the league that have had success [haven't done that]. Even when they have a great player like [Panthers star Aleksander] Barkov, [Anton] Lundell filled that spot most of the time."</p><p>Oliver Moore's defense was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/23/blackhawks-oliver-moore-nhl-rookie-performance-connor-bedard-jimmy-snuggerud" target="_blank" >better than expected</a> this season, but his injury prevented the Hawks from getting a substantial look at him in a checking role.</p><p>Ryan Greene's defense was acclaimed coming out of college, when he was heralded as a Dickinson-type player, but he also hasn't gotten much experience in an NHL checking role this season due to his unexpected chemistry with Bedard.</p><p>Blashill mentioned Sunday wanting to give Greene some time at center (presumably third-line center) down the stretch, but after he opted Tuesday to bump Burakovsky instead of Greene off Bedard's line to make room for Frondell, he was noncommittal about the Greene-at-center idea.</p><p>Landon Slaggert has solidified his lineup spot this season with decent defense and penalty-killing, but it's unclear if his upside exceeds that of "fourth-line utility guy."</p><p>Sacha Boisvert, who will likely debut Thursday against the Flyers, is another candidate — as is AJ Spellacy, who will compete for an NHL job next fall. But although their physicality and grittiness will be welcome in the lineup, those traits don't necessarily translate to defensive effectiveness.</p><p>This question could linger for a while.</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: West Suburban Medical Center closing temporarily as it runs out of cash to pay employees
entries.summary: <p>West Suburban Medical Center is temporarily shutting its doors and furloughing "many" employees as it struggles to pay staff, according to the Oak Park hospital's owner, Manoj Prasad. </p><p>Prasad, the CEO of Resilience Healthcare, said in an email Wednesday to the Sun-Times that the hospital's emergency room, inpatient units and clinics are closing, "effective immediately." He blamed the hospital's new electronic medical record system for the payroll issues. </p><p>"Exactly one year ago, West Suburban Medical Center transitioned to a new electronic medical record system that has never functioned correctly," Prasad wrote in the email. "Despite repairs having been made, the billing and collection system still has serious problems. The result is that West Suburban has managed to keep operating and serving its patients for a full year on a fraction of the revenue it needs to provide the services needed by its community."</p><p>He did not say when the safety net hospital would reopen. </p><p>This comes seven months after Resilience Healthcare shuttered its other facility, Weiss Memorial Hospital in Chicago's Uptown neighborhood.</p><p>Prasad sent a message to staff announcing the closure Wednesday. The email, obtained by the Sun-Times, said "many" employees will be furloughed. </p><p>The email explained that the hospital has been surviving on around "10% to 15% of our normal income" for the past year.</p><p>“While you have been focused on healing the sick, several of us have been focused on a different crisis that started exactly one year ago when we transitioned to the new EMR,” Prasad wrote to staff, referring to the electronic medical record system </p><p>He said “at least half” of the hospital’s work is not getting billed and it will take "months of focused hard work" to resolve the billing issue. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-0a0000" name="image-0a0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" width="490" height="275" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Resilience Healthcare CEO Dr. Manoj Prasad speaking to reporters at West Suburban Medical Center in August 2025." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8327a6e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5998x3366+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F88%2F18%2F1527e2ae4983a6548c6a82e758f3%2Fweissfolo-080925-19.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3791147/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5998x3366+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F88%2F18%2F1527e2ae4983a6548c6a82e758f3%2Fweissfolo-080925-19.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8327a6e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5998x3366+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F88%2F18%2F1527e2ae4983a6548c6a82e758f3%2Fweissfolo-080925-19.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Resilience Healthcare CEO Dr. Manoj Prasad speaking to reporters at West Suburban Medical Center last year.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In Prasad's email to the Sun-Times, he said hospital leadership "developed and deployed a labor-intensive manual work-around to bill and collect revenue." While that strategy has been effective, he said that process will take time to fully catch up on billing. </p><p>"This remediation plan has been successful in beginning to bring in revenue needed to keep the hospital operational. However, this process will take time to produce the necessary results," Prasad wrote. "At the same time, normal operating expenses need to be met, even while prices are climbing."</p><p>Because of that, the hospital will "temporarily cease providing all patient care services through the Emergency Room, the hospital and clinics, effective immediately. Once the revenue needed to fund operations is received, services will be resumed," he wrote. </p><p>The newspaper Wednesday Journal of Oak Park and River Forest was the first to report West Suburban's closure.</p><p>Resilience Healthcare's other facility, Weiss Memorial Hospital, closed last August <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2025/07/25/weiss-memorial-hospital-loses-license-ac-uptown" target="_blank" >after losing Medicare and Medicaid funding</a>. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2025/07/29/weiss-memorial-emergency-room-unsafe-patients-illinois-public-health" target="_blank" >Weiss had been plagued with issues</a> for many months, including a broken air conditioning system and a dysfunctional emergency room.</p><p>At the time, Prasad <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2025/08/08/weiss-memorial-hospital-emergency-room-in-uptown-closes" target="_blank" >warned in a news conference</a> that West Suburban could meet a similar fate.</p><p><i>This is a developing story. Check back for updates.</i></p><p><i>Contributing: Kristen Schorsch </i></p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2025/08/08/weiss-memorial-hospital-emergency-room-in-uptown-closes" >As Weiss Memorial Hospital closes, its owner blames previous owners for financial woes</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Kaitlin Washburn

entries.title: PAWS Chicago recibe a más de 40 perros tras la incautación masiva de mascotas en Los Ángeles
entries.summary: <p>Docenas de perros de California fueron recibidos por PAWS Chicago el martes después de que una investigación por abuso animal llevara a una de las mayores incautaciones de mascotas en Los Ángeles, lo que resultó en refugios sobrepoblados, según informó el grupo de Chicago.</p><p>Los perros recibidos por PAWS no forman parte de los animales incautados en Los Ángeles, pero son parte del efecto dominó resultante.</p><p>Todo comenzó el viernes, cuando más de 300 perros y gatos fueron rescatados de un refugio animal en Los Ángeles tras quejas sobre las condiciones inhumanas de vida.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-720000" name="module-720000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Esos animales incautados se consideran evidencia y, por ahora, no son elegibles para adopción. Pero dado que están siendo mantenidos en refugios locales, "las mascotas que ya están en estos refugios no tienen a dónde ir y corren el riesgo de ser sacrificadas", dijo Sarah McDonald, directora asociada de relaciones con los medios para PAWS Chicago.</p><p>Y así fue como PAWS terminó con los 41 perros de California que llegaron al PAWS Kocourek Medical Center en el vecindario de La Villita el martes por la tarde. Entre ellos: una madre y sus ocho cachorros.</p><p>Paws for Life K9 Rescue en Los Ángeles voló a los perros a Gary, Indiana, donde fueron recogidos por voluntarios de Chicago a eso de las 3 p.m. el martes.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-e57532" name="image-e57532"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/64b664b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2Fda%2Fe09bb4b04ddb8e325891b018d565%2Fpawsrescue-260325-1-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/32354de/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2Fda%2Fe09bb4b04ddb8e325891b018d565%2Fpawsrescue-260325-1-1.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/18d4b18/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2Fda%2Fe09bb4b04ddb8e325891b018d565%2Fpawsrescue-260325-1-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Docenas de perros y cachorros llegaron al Centro Médico Kocourek de PAWS Chicago y a la Lurie Clinic en el vecindario de La Villita el martes. Formaban parte de una transferencia de mascotas de emergencia que se volvió necesaria debido a la sobrepoblación en algunos refugios de animales de Los Ángeles, causada por la incautación de cientos de otros perros durante una investigación por abuso animal." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/18d4b18/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2Fda%2Fe09bb4b04ddb8e325891b018d565%2Fpawsrescue-260325-1-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/dcf9c8e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2Fda%2Fe09bb4b04ddb8e325891b018d565%2Fpawsrescue-260325-1-1.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/18d4b18/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2Fda%2Fe09bb4b04ddb8e325891b018d565%2Fpawsrescue-260325-1-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Docenas de perros y cachorros llegaron al Centro Médico Kocourek de PAWS Chicago y a la Lurie Clinic en el vecindario de La Villita el martes. Formaban parte de una transferencia de mascotas de emergencia que se volvió necesaria debido a la sobrepoblación en algunos refugios de animales de Los Ángeles, causada por la incautación de cientos de otros perros durante una investigación por abuso animal.<br></p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>A los perros se les iba a dar la cena antes de sus evaluaciones médicas y vacunaciones el miércoles, mientras que los cachorros estaban siendo preparandos para ser esterilizados o castrados, dijo McDonald.</p><p>En una <a class="Link" href="https://www.facebook.com/share/p/176Gdwtogg/" target="_blank" >publicación en redes sociales</a>, la Pasadena Humane Society dijo, “Las condiciones en el lugar eran deplorables, y algunos animales necesitaban atención médica urgente".</p><p>El Departamento de Cuidado y Control Animal del Condado de Los Ángeles no respondió de inmediato a una solicitud de comentarios.</p><p>PAWS Chicago tiende a recibir grandes grupos de animales en momentos de crisis, como los huracanes Ian y Milton, aunque McDonald mencionó que tales eventos parecen estar ocurriendo con más frecuencia, y el grupo está recibiendo incluso más perros de lo habitual.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-a2c108" name="image-a2c108"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" Los animales formaban parte de 41 que fueron trasladados en avión desde Los Ángeles en una transferencia de mascotas de emergencia." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4671df5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9f%2F6d%2F72613f0247919405dd8027118980%2Fpawsrescue-260325-38.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7a6ae4e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9f%2F6d%2F72613f0247919405dd8027118980%2Fpawsrescue-260325-38.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4671df5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9f%2F6d%2F72613f0247919405dd8027118980%2Fpawsrescue-260325-38.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Los voluntarios de PAWS Chicago procesan cachorros y perros el martes. Los animales formaban parte de 41 que fueron trasladados en avión desde Los Ángeles en una transferencia de mascotas de emergencia.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Celene Mielcarek, directora de programas de PAWS Chicago, dijo que llega en un momento crítico para la adopción de mascotas en Chicago también. La primavera significa temporada de cría para los refugios, lo que lleva a una capacidad aún más reducida.</p><p>Afortunadamente, McDonald dijo que los residentes de Chicago tienden a no dejar que los cachorros, especialmente los más pequeños, permanezcan mucho tiempo en los refugios. Instó a las personas a tomar el evento de crisis como inspiración para acoger o adoptar cachorros, especialmente para aquellos que buscan activarse a medida que el clima se calienta, de verdad, esta vez.</p><p>Esperan que los próximos eventos de adopción, junto con etiquetas en el sitio web que promocionan a los cachorros de Los Ángeles, aseguren que encuentren hogares rápidamente.</p><p>“Cada hogar de acogida que tenemos libera un espacio en nuestro centro médico”, señaló McDonald. “Cada adopción salva dos vidas porque libera más espacio”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center>
