entries.title: Mayor Johnson vetoes plan to freeze subminimum wage for tipped workers
entries.summary: <p>Mayor Brandon Johnson on Wednesday used his third veto to bury an ordinance that <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/03/18/city-council-freeze-subminimum-wage-phase-out-tipped-workers-restaurants-brandon-johnson" >would have frozen</a> the hourly pay of Chicago’s tipped workers at 76% of the minimum wage, and he’s likely to make it stick unless there’s a compromise to give struggling restaurants a short break.</p><p>Last week, the City Council voted 30-18 to maintain the subminimum wage to throw an economic lifeline to struggling Chicago restaurants, four votes short of the 34 votes needed to override the mayor’s veto.</p><p>At a news conference at Let’s Eat Live Restaurant in the Woodlawn neighborhood that had the feel of a campaign rally, Johnson said working people will “always have a fighter on the fifth floor in Brandon Johnson,” adding there has “never been a right time ever to roll back the rights and gains of women — particularly women of color,” but it’s "especially tone deaf and disturbing” now in the midst of an affordability crisis.</p><p>“Families are struggling under the weight of rising costs, rent, groceries, child care — and wages have not kept up with these rising costs. At a time when people are fighting just to stay afloat, you had individuals who claim to stand up for working people, as Democrats take money out of the pockets of working people,” the mayor said.</p><p>Illinois Restaurant Association President Sam Toia is targeting nine alderpersons who voted against the freeze despite having heavy concentrations of restaurants in their wards, in hopes of convincing at least four of them to change their votes and override the mayor’s veto.</p><p>They include Alds. Julia Ramirez (12th) and Jeylu Gutierrez (14th), whose wards include the Archer Avenue commercial strip, and Ald. Jason Ervin (28th), whose West Side ward includes half of the Taylor Street restaurant district.</p><p>Other Council members Toia is lobbying include Daniel La Spata (1st), who represents restaurant-heavy Bucktown and Wicker Park; Lamont Robinson (4th), whose ward includes Hyde Park restaurants and both Andre Vasquez (40th) and Matt Martin (47th), whose North Side wards include Lincoln Square and North Center.</p><p>“It’s a heavy lift. I’m very pragmatic. I’ve been around. But all I can do is keep working keep pushing. We’ve got a full-court press. I’m gonna fight up until the last minute. I have between now and April 15th” when the City Council meets again to consider an override, Toia said.</p><p>“My pitch to these people is, we know that 496 restaurants closed in the first half of 2025. And if they take another hit on labor costs, this could literally put a lot of our independent family-owned restaurants out of business.”</p><p>The Sun-Times contacted several of those alderpersons, and none said they were entertaining the idea of changing their vote to support an override of the mayor’s veto.</p><p>Robinson, La Spata and Vasquez said they are open to working with the mayor’s office on a compromise that would give restaurants a one-year break from the phase-out.</p><p>An ordinance to do just that has already been introduced by newly appointed Ald. Walter R. Burnett (27th), whose Near West Side ward includes the bustling Fulton Market district. Johnson rejected that one-year freeze.</p><p>“We have to figure out how to move this forward to benefit both sides. The workers need wages. But we also have to make sure we’re supportive of small businesses that are the backbone of communities like mine. These folks hire a lot of people, we have to support them,” Robinson said.</p><p></p><p>Vasquez said he also favored a shorter reprieve for restaurants that are already required to make up the difference for those servers and bartenders whose tips and subminimum wage do not match Chicago’s minimum wage, which now stands at $16.60 an hour and rises every July 1.</p><p>“There’s ways to talk [about] maybe an exemption for smaller businesses that are separate from these larger corporations that are doing just fine. Maybe doing a pause with a finite amount of time,” Vasquez said.</p><p></p><p>La Spata said he supports a one-year freeze over an override because he believes the economic squeeze on Chicago restaurants stem “much more from the economic policies of the Trump administration than they have anything to do with paying their servers a dollar more this year.”</p><p>“I also think it’s unfair to essentially scapegoat servers for those economic costs,” La Spata said.</p><p>Ramirez said she is leaning toward upholding the mayor’s veto “based off the workers who have organized, the stories I’ve learned from them and the conversations I’ve had with restaurant owners” in her ward.</p><p>For his part, Johnson urged Chicagoans to call their alderperson and “let them know that this veto makes sense for the people of Chicago.</p><p>"If you believe that women… deserve equal pay for their work that they are doing," Johnson said, "call your alderman and tell them that you agree with that.”</p><p></p><p> </p><p></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-960000" entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: Mayor Johnson unveils 'Abolish ICE' snowplow
entries.summary: <p>Mayor Brandon Johnson and city officials unveiled Wednesday the “Abolish ICE” snowplow, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/02/23/abolish-ice-among-winners-in-chicagos-you-name-a-snowplow-contest" target="_blank" >the winner of this year’s snowplow naming contest</a>, which drew a record number of submissions.</p><p>The mayor said the winning snowplow name is a message to the federal government that U.S Immigration and Customs Enforcement isn’t welcome in Chicago.</p><p>But the unveiling in West Town came as Chicago has again become a focal point of America’s debate over immigration policies following last week’s fatal shooting of an 18-year-old college student.</p><p>Loyola University Chicago freshman Sheridan Gorman was shot and killed near the Rogers Park campus. The alleged gunman, Jose G. Medina, is a Venezuelan immigrant who had been taken into custody by U.S. Border Patrol in May 2023, released, then arrested again months later on shoplifting charges.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/24/chicago-crime-trump-pritzker-bovino-immigration-midway-blitz-deport-immigration" >Sheridan Gorman’s killing puts Chicago back at the center of America’s bitter fight over immigration</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/24/heres-what-to-know-killing-sheridan-gorman" >What to know about the killing of Sheridan Gorman</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>One heckler at Wednesday’s event called out the mayor for unveiling the snowplow less than a week after Gorman’s death.</p><p>"You're making a joke out of Sheridan Gorman!" the heckler shouted. "Shame on you!"</p><p>“My condolences to the family of Sheridan. This is a terrible tragedy,” Johnson said. “And what I’ve said from the very beginning, I’m going to continue to use every single tool that’s available to me to protect the residents of the city of Chicago. And this tragedy is not going to deter us from our work. In fact, it’s going to challenge us all to double down on our efforts to ensure that we are protecting every single individual across neighborhoods.”</p><p>The fourth annual “You Name a Snowplow” contest returned in December and yielded 13,300 submissions. The slogan “Abolish ICE,” taking aim at the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agency, was submitted around 9,300 times, or about 70% of entries.</p><p>The flood of “Abolish ICE” entries followed several tense months in Chicago during which federal immigration agents caravanned around the city making arrests and deploying tear gas and other chemical irritants at protesters. Federal agents fatally shot one man, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/09/15/silverio-villegas-gonzalez-fatally-shot-by-ice-agents-touched-the-lives-of-so-many-loved-ones-say" target="_blank" >Silverio Villegas Gonzalez</a>, and wounded a woman, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/03/marimar-martinez-shot-border-patrol-chicago-speaks-out-after-good-pretti-deaths-i-am-their-voice" target="_blank" >Marimar Martinez</a>, in separate incidents.</p><p>Protests occurred regularly around the city and at the ICE processing facility in suburban Broadview.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/23/ice-agents-ohare-airport-partial-government-shutdown" target="_blank" >ICE agents were also deployed</a> to airports across the country, including to O'Hare Airport, on Monday amid a partial government shutdown that has left TSA officers working without pay for weeks.</p><p>“This name derives from our city’s legacy of standing up for justice, dignity and the rights of all people no matter where they come from,” Johnson said. “I want to take this moment to reiterate that Chicago does not want ICE on our streets, in our airports nor in our city. Chicago believes in abolishing ICE.”</p><p><i>Contributing: Zubaer Khan</i></p>
entries.author: Kade Heather

entries.title: Six Big Ten teams, five Sweet 16 games and one intrepid writer's picks
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports" target="_blank" >Big Ten</a> has six teams in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/25/illinois-sweet-16-houston-illini-cougars-ncaa-tournament-march-madness" target="_blank" >Sweet 16 of the men’s NCAA Tournament</a>. That’s a lot, even for an 18-team conference.</p><p>Illinois, Iowa and Nebraska are still alive in the South Region, along with Purdue in the West, Michigan in the Midwest and Michigan State in the East. An all-Big Ten Final Four, anyone? It’s possible, but a huge stretch for a league without a national title since 2000.</p><p>How many will get through to the Elite Eight? My picks:</p><h3><b>South: No. 3 seed Illinois vs. No. 2 seed Houston</b></h3><p><b>TV: </b>9:05 p.m. Thursday, TBS, truTV.</p><p><b>Line: </b>Houston by 2½.</p><p><b>Storyline: </b>The Illini are elite offensively, but the Cougars are every bit as strong defensively. The Illini are bigger, the Cougars more agile. Freshman point guards Keaton Wagler (Illinois) and Kingston Flemings (Houston) are the names at the top of the marquee.</p><p><b>Pick: </b>Houston, 80-75.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/25/illinois-sweet-16-houston-illini-cougars-ncaa-tournament-march-madness" >Illinois’ Sweet 16 date with Houston — the team and the city — is cause for some perspective</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/22/illinois-ncaa-houston-cougars-illini-sweet-16-march-madness" >Illinois’ NCAA path blocked by Houston, a powerhouse that will be hard to handle in Sweet 16</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/22/big-ten-packed-sweet-16-the-dream-scenario-for-championship-starved-conference" target="_blank" >Is Big Ten-packed Sweet 16 the dream scenario for championship-starved conference?</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p><h3><b>South: No. 9 Iowa vs. No. 4 Nebraska</b></h3><p><b>TV: </b>6:30 p.m. Thursday, CBS 2.</p><p><b>Line: </b>Nebraska by 1½.</p><p><b>Storyline: </b>The Hawkeyes haven’t been to the Elite Eight since 1987. These Huskers have notched Nebraska’s only two tournament wins ever. Hey, it’s Fred Hoiberg!</p><p><b>Pick: </b>Iowa, 68-66.</p><h3><b>West: No. 2 Purdue vs. No. 11 Texas</b></h3><p><b>TV: </b>6:10 p.m. Thursday, CBS 2.</p><p><b>Line: </b>Purdue by 7½.</p><p><b>Storyline: </b>The Longhorns are rolling, having begun their journey in the First Four, but nobody’s hotter than the Boilermakers.</p><p><b>Pick: </b>Purdue, 91-81.</p><h3><b>Midwest: No. 1 Michigan vs. No. 4 Alabama</b></h3><p><b>TV info: </b>6:35 p.m. Friday, TBS, truTV.</p><p><b>Line: </b>Michigan by 9½.</p><p><b>Storyline: </b>The Wolverines are in the danger zone against the Crimson Tide, who lead the nation in scoring (91.6 ppg) and dazzled in a second-round blowout of Texas Tech.</p><p><b>Pick: </b>Michigan, 90-84.</p><h3><b>West: No. 3 Michigan State vs. No. 2 UConn</b></h3><p><b>TV info: </b>8:45 p.m. Friday, CBS 2.</p><p><b>Line: </b>UConn by 1½.</p><p><b>Storyline: </b>The intensity on the floor will be through the roof, and that’s just coaches Dan Hurley and Tom Izzo. Those two might have to settle it with a snarl-off.</p><p><b>Pick: </b>Michigan State, 70-69.</p>
entries.author: Steve Greenberg

entries.title: Teen charged in armed sexual assault of 15-year-old girl in Albany Park
entries.summary: <p>A Chicago teen has been charged in an armed sexual assault last November in Albany Park, according to the Cook County state’s attorney’s office.</p><p>Gerson Castillo Flores, 17, is charged as an adult and faces aggravated criminal sexual assault with firearm and kidnapping while armed with a firearm, according to Chicago police. Flores, who has no prior criminal history, was ordered detained Wednesday by Judge Luciano Panici.</p><p>Flores was arrested Tuesday. While in police custody, he allegedly confessed to touching the girl and shooting at her November 21, prosecutors said. He told police the encounter was consensual, but said the girl may have been scared because he was holding her at gunpoint, prosecutors said.</p><p>The girl and her friend, both 15, had been walking around the Rockwell train station at 9:20 p.m. when they saw two people — including Flores, who was wearing a ski mask — during which point they crossed the street, prosecutors said. Flores told police after being arrested that his friend, referred to in court documents as a co-defendant, said, “Let’s steal them," prosecutors said.</p><p>The girls ran, and one fell, being cornered by Flores’ friend, prosecutors said. Flores then allegedly chased the other girl, pressed the barrel of a gun against her chest and forced her into the alley in the 2900 block of West Wilson Avenue where he assaulted her, prosecutors said.</p><p>Several witnesses in the neighborhood arrived and shooed the men away, prosecutors said.</p><p>Flores told police the weapon was an airgun, though a muzzle flash was caught on surveillance camera when he allegedly fired it at the victim and others as he was walking away, prosecutors said.</p><p>Flores is due again in court April 15.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: More César Chavez murals, memorials taken down as Illinois Senate honors Dolores Huerta
entries.summary: <p>Murals and memorials of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2026/03/18/allegations-of-abuse-by-cesar-chavez-sadden-shock-chicagos-latino-community" target="_blank" >César Chavez</a> are continuing to come down across Chicago after allegations emerged last week that the Latino civil rights activist groomed and sexually assaulted girls and women.</p><p>The allegations were revealed in a New York Times investigation published March 18.</p><p>Some artworks are being repainted with murals of Dolores Huerta, the longtime ally of Chavez in the labor rights movement. Huerta, 95, told the New York Times that Chavez had sexually assaulted her.</p><p>The Illinois Senate adopted a resolution Wednesday honoring Huerta and declaring April 10 as “Dolores Huerta Day” in Illinois.</p><p>“In recent months, Dolores Huerta has shown profound resilience and courage in sharing her own experience of harm, doing so in order to uplift the stories of countless women whose voices were overlooked or silenced,” the resolution states.</p><p>One of the resolution's sponsors, State Sen. Celina Villanueva, D-Chicago, said she is also working on a plan to rescind César Chavez Day, which is next week on March 31st.</p><p>At Cafe Tola in Lake View, a Chavez mural outside the restaurant’s building was replaced this week with a painting of Huerta, who coined the phrase, “Sí, se puede,” which loosely translates to, “Yes, we can.”</p><p>“We are deeply saddened by this news, yet we stand in unwavering support and admiration for Dolores Huerta and every woman who has found the courage to come forward,” Cafe Tola said in a social media post. “This moment is powerful — proof that truth cannot be silenced and that it is never too late to reclaim your voice. We honor that strength.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d10000" Chavez’s labor rights movement has been covered with black tape at the Haymarket Memorial. According to the Chicago Federation of Labor, the organization is planning to fully remove it.</p><p>“We will be discussing with all our partners on how to best honor both the survivors of Cesar Chavez’s horrific abuse and the workers that were betrayed by Chavez,” a spokesperson for the Chicago Federation of Labor said in a statement.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Black tape covers up a plaque (left) that commemorates the 30th anniversary of César Chavez’s death on the base of the Haymarket Memorial in the West Loop, Wednesday, March 25, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Last week, Bob Reiter, president of the Chicago Federation of Labor, said the allegations that Chavez sexually assaulted children, women, workers and labor organizers “is a betrayal to our movement.”</p><p>Chavez, who died in 1993, has long been revered in Chicago’s Latino community. In addition to numerous murals of him across the city, a public elementary school in Back of the Yards and a post office in Pilsen are both named after him.</p><p>The school, César E. Chavez Multicultural Academic Center, has initiated the process that could lead to a new name. It is receiving community feedback about a possible new name, according to Chicago Public Schools officials. Any recommendation would need approval from the Local School Council and the school board.</p><p>Another school in Pilsen, Peter Cooper Dual Language Academy, has a mosaic of Chavez and Huerta side by side. It’s unclear if the school plans to remove the mosaic of Chavez.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div </div></div><p>The Sun-Times was the first to report that Chavez’s face had been <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/03/20/leaders-at-southwest-side-elementary-school-named-for-cesar-chavez-moving-to-potentially-rename-school" target="_blank" >painted over last week</a> on the “Libertad” mural across a long wall at Barrett Park in Pilsen.</p><p>A splotch of green paint initially covered the portion where Chavez was depicted, but Park District officials have since painted over that with a continuation of the mural’s background design, a Park District spokesperson said.</p><p>Other historical figures on the mural, including Mexican artist Frida Kahlo, Malcolm X, Rosa Parks, and the Rev. Park District takes recent allegations of misconduct by Cesar Chavez seriously … and are conducting a district-wide review of any other park features that may honor him,” a Park District spokesperson said previously. “Where appropriate, we will take further action consistent with our values and standards.”</p><p>The Park District has not identified any other murals or memorials of Chavez on any of its properties, the spokesperson said Wednesday.</p><p><i>Contributing: Isabela Nieto</i></p>
entries.author: Kade Heather

entries.title: Route 66 centennial celebrated with new sign at Navy Pier
entries.summary: <p>U.S. Route 66 is a cultural landmark that holds the rich history of America’s automotive and commercial boom, and the memories of countless family roadtrips. </p><p>The storied highway, which spans 2,400 miles between Chicago to California, is celebrating its centennial this year. Chicago honored the landmark Wednesday by unveiling a new sign at Navy Pier outside of Harry Caray’s Tavern.</p><p>Vintage cars surrounded the sign as city leaders and preservationists gathered to mark the milestone.</p><p>One of the speakers, Bill Thomas, said he was 8-years-old during his first trip on Route 66 to Disneyland. </p><p>He recalled the grueling temperatures and "adventure" of it all. That's something he appreciates now as commissioner of the U.S. Route 66 Centennial Commission.</p><p>“We drove through the desert and it was 100 degrees," said Thomas, 71. "This is part of the experience."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <bsp-carousel class="Carousel Enhancement" data-align-center> <a class="AnchorLink" aria-label="Previous"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-left"></use></svg> </button> <button class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-next" type="button" aria-label="Next"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-right"></use></svg> </button> </div> </div></div> </bsp-carousel> </div> </div><p>He said Route 66, commissioned in 1926, gave Americans the opportunity to be free. That meant a better, higher quality of life, he said.</p><p>The officially recognized start of the iconic highway in Illinois has moved at least twice before. There are multiple signs honoring those locations. One of them stands at Adams Street and Wabash Avenue.</p><p>The new signage at Navy Pier was chosen by the Route 66 Commission and city of Chicago chose Navy Pier, tying the city even more closely to the highway’s national story.</p><p>Route 66's path was first laid out by indigenous groups who used animal footprints to guide the road. They created trade routes that directed the path we know today.</p><p>What was once “America’s Main Street” was decommissioned and removed from the U.S. highway system in 1985. As commercial needs grew, and the nation adopted what’s now the interstate highway system, Route 66 became an open road for motor enthusiasts and tourism.</p><p>“I have yet to find a demographic group or culture who doesn't have a connection with Route 66,” Thomas said.</p><p>But not every group could experience Route 66 in the same way.</p><p>Black travelers had to avoid sundown towns, and leaned on a publication called “<a class="Link" href="https://nmaahc.si.edu/object/nmaahc_2021.37" target="_blank" >The Green Book</a>” to locate lodging, dining and gas stations where they could patronize. Thomas said hardly any of those original structures still exist.</p><p>During the segregation era, permitted locations for Black travelers were sometimes half a mile off of the road. After severe dust storms, many landmarks along the route were destroyed.</p><p>Preservationists like Thomas are working to restore the road’s legacy. Along with the route’s tourist attractions, cultural staples like the famous song “Route 66” by Bobby Troup and the Disney Pixar movie Cars keep the story of Route 66 alive across generations.</p>
entries.author: Somer Van Benton | WBEZ

entries.title: Foundation's role a mystery as Lincoln artifacts are poised to be auctioned off
entries.summary: <p>Artifacts tied to Honest Abe hit the auction block Thursday, with some bearing a striking resemblance to items from a vast collection that a private foundation once acquired for display at the state-run Abraham Lincoln Presidential Library and Museum.</p><p>The sale at the Chicago-based Freeman’s auction house includes rare manuscripts, documents, and other memorabilia related to the life and legacy of the 16th president.</p><p>Some notable items include a bust of Lincoln biographer Carl Sandburg previously owned by Marilyn Monroe, another bust of Ulysses Grant, and locks of hair purportedly taken from the head of Lincoln assassin John Wilkes Booth after he was fatally shot during a manhunt.</p><p>These items were once owned by wealthy West Coast historian and Lincoln collector Louise Taper, who sold her collection in 2007 to what now is the Lincoln Presidential Foundation for $23 million with hopes her artifacts would become state property and forever displayed at the state-run presidential museum in Lincoln’s hometown.</p><p>Instead, many of her iconic pieces of Lincolniana have landed in the hands of unknown buyers after an auction last May raised nearly $8 million, and more may be going that way imminently.</p><p>Taper was sharply critical of that last auction, which included the sale of blood-stained gloves from Lincoln's coat pocket the night he was assassinated. She said she was “appalled” at the foundation’s willingness to sell off pieces of her prized collection.</p><p>“My intent was for these historic items to reside in a place for the public to enjoy and learn from,” she told WBEZ last spring.</p><p>Neither the foundation nor Freeman’s would confirm whether Thursday’s auction included more items from Taper’s former collection and, if so, what the rationale was behind selling more Lincoln artifacts.</p><p>All told, 190 items are for sale, including a handful of relics tied to George Washington, Benjamin Franklin, Alexander Hamilton and Theodore Roosevelt. But Lincoln artifacts represent the bulk of the sale.</p><p>“These materials form a powerful narrative of vision and sacrifice — of founders and preservers — presented in a landmark auction honoring the enduring legacy of America’s political leaders,” Freeman’s wrote in an online introduction to the auction.</p><p>Unlike at last May's auction, Freeman’s sale bill this time did not identify the foundation as the owner of Lincoln items up for sale. But WBEZ identified several items that appear to be identical to those in the original Taper acquisition.</p><p>The station cross-referenced dozens of items’ images and descriptions on the Freeman’s sale bill with an original inventory and photographs of Taper artifacts held by the presidential museum.</p><p>A foundation representative Wednesday would not verify whether it was selling off more of its Lincoln collection, though his written statement stopped far short of an outright denial.</p><p>"For many years, artifacts and letters associated with Abraham Lincoln have attracted a lot of public attention and interest. As a general practice, the Lincoln Presidential Foundation does not comment on secondary-market activity or sales," foundation spokesman Nick Kalm said.</p><p>Marsha Malinowski, a nationally known manuscripts expert based in New York City who was a senior executive at the Sotheby’s auction house for 26 years, said if Taper's items are being sold, the absence of references to the foundation in this auction may stem from her earlier criticism.</p><p>“There was a bit of a flurry of press for the first sale with all the material,” she said.</p><p>The foundation has said items from Taper’s collection would be sold if outstanding debt could not be paid down.</p><p>In 2018, the organization went to the statehouse to lobby for help in offsetting the $9.2 million it owed then on its outstanding acquisition debt.</p><p>The foundation insisted without the state’s help, collection items would need to be auctioned to meet an October 2019 loan deadline.</p><p>The foundation's efforts to acquire state funding to cover the remaining debt proved unsuccessful, but it did manage to get its loan refinanced for an additional three years.</p><p>By 2022, a foundation spokesperson confirmed that the not-for-profit still had a debt exceeding $8 million related to the purchase of the Taper collection.</p><p>That year also coincided with the end of a 15-year agreement between the Lincoln Presidential Foundation and the ALPLM that allowed for the display of Taper artifacts at the museum. The collection’s 1,540 items were then removed from display that fall.</p><p>In May 2025, 144 items from the collection went up for auction, including a swatch of fabric from a coat Lincoln wore when he was assassinated, and his earliest known handwriting sample from 1824.</p>
entries.author: Sam Naftzger | WBEZ

entries.title: 19 things to do in Chicago March 26-April 1
entries.summary: <h3>Theater</h3><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-de62eaa0-265b-11f1-9e2e-5b40c39add4b"><li>The Tony Award-winning musical <b>“The Wiz,”</b> William F. Brown and Charlie Smalls’ twist on “The Wizard of Oz,” re-imagines Dorothy’s journey with soul, gospel, rock and ’70s funk. Dana Cimone stars; Schele Williams directs. From March 31-April 5 at Nederlander Theatre, 24 W. Randolph St. Tickets: $43-$121. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://broadwayinchicago.com" target="_blank" ><u>broadwayinchicago.com</u></a>.</li></ul><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-400000" name="image-400000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e488799/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4171x2341+0+182/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F89%2F01%2F88ee75e7410a8d4087351d90e12d%2Fma-rainey-rehearsals-photo-by-justin-barbin.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b099279/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4171x2341+0+182/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F89%2F01%2F88ee75e7410a8d4087351d90e12d%2Fma-rainey-rehearsals-photo-by-justin-barbin.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/65e5cc8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4171x2341+0+182/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F89%2F01%2F88ee75e7410a8d4087351d90e12d%2Fma-rainey-rehearsals-photo-by-justin-barbin.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Ma Rainey Rehearsals - photo by Justin Barbin.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/65e5cc8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4171x2341+0+182/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F89%2F01%2F88ee75e7410a8d4087351d90e12d%2Fma-rainey-rehearsals-photo-by-justin-barbin.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2c1f87c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4171x2341+0+182/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F89%2F01%2F88ee75e7410a8d4087351d90e12d%2Fma-rainey-rehearsals-photo-by-justin-barbin.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/65e5cc8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4171x2341+0+182/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F89%2F01%2F88ee75e7410a8d4087351d90e12d%2Fma-rainey-rehearsals-photo-by-justin-barbin.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Director Chuck Smith (left) and associate/music director Harry Lennix of “Ma Rainey’s Black Bottom”</p></figcaption></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-de62eaa1-265b-11f1-9e2e-5b40c39add4b"><li>August Wilson’s <b>“Ma Rainey’s Black Bottom”</b> is set in 1927 Chicago as “The Mother of the Blues” takes her time getting ready to record. Meanwhile tensions rise as the waiting musicians recount tales of rage, joy, betrayal and faith. Chuck Smith directs with music direction by Harry J. Lennix. From March 28-April 26 at Goodman Theatre, 170 N. Dearborn St. Tickets: $34-$104. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://goodmantheatre.org" target="_blank" ><u>goodmantheatre.org</u></a>.</li><li>The Artistic Home presents <b>“The Sugar Wife,”</b> Elizabeth Kuti’s drama about a Quaker woman who is torn between her work with the city’s poor and her husband’s business built on the exploitation of the poor. Kevin Hagan directs. From March 28-May 3 at Theater Wit, 1229 W. Belmont Ave. Tickets: $35. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://theatrewit.org" target="_blank" ><u>theatrewit.org</u></a>.</li><li>Idle Muse Theatre stages Robert Kauzlaric’s adaptation of Alexandre Dumas’ novel, <b>“The Three Musketeers,”</b> a swashbuckling tale of intrigue, adventure and fellowship. Evan Jackson directs. From March 26-April 25 at The Edge Off-Broadway Theater, 1133 W. Catalpa Ave. Tickets: $30. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://idlemuse.org" target="_blank" ><u>idlemuse.org</u></a>.</li></ul><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-4d0000" name="image-4d0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c28d9ce/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5926x3326+0+313/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fab%2Fb9%2Fcf2c8afe40d4b7644086ac76d97a%2Fgary-houston-photo-by-sandy-morris.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7297594/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5926x3326+0+313/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fab%2Fb9%2Fcf2c8afe40d4b7644086ac76d97a%2Fgary-houston-photo-by-sandy-morris.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/83a7675/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5926x3326+0+313/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fab%2Fb9%2Fcf2c8afe40d4b7644086ac76d97a%2Fgary-houston-photo-by-sandy-morris.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Gary Houston - photo by Sandy Morris.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/83a7675/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5926x3326+0+313/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fab%2Fb9%2Fcf2c8afe40d4b7644086ac76d97a%2Fgary-houston-photo-by-sandy-morris.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ec889ff/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5926x3326+0+313/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fab%2Fb9%2Fcf2c8afe40d4b7644086ac76d97a%2Fgary-houston-photo-by-sandy-morris.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/83a7675/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5926x3326+0+313/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fab%2Fb9%2Fcf2c8afe40d4b7644086ac76d97a%2Fgary-houston-photo-by-sandy-morris.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Gary Houston </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Sandy Morris</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-de62eaa2-265b-11f1-9e2e-5b40c39add4b"><li>Her Story Theatre presents <b>“The Official Biography,”</b> Kurt McGinnis Brown’s drama about a young Black journalist (Shelby Marie Edwards) assigned to interview a once-successful white novelist (Gary Houston) and write a scathing expose. From March 28-April 19 at The Den Theatre, 1331 N. Milwaukee Ave. Tickets: $40. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://thedentheatre.com" target="_blank" ><u>thedentheatre.com</u></a>.</li><li>Second Site stages Rachel DuBose’s <b>“The Beauty Project,”</b> an immersive theatrical experience that speaks to the issues of identity, belonging and quality of life. entries.author: Mary Houlihan

entries.title: Illinois' Sweet 16 date with Houston — the team and the city — is cause for some perspective
entries.summary: <p>It’s the time of year in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports" target="_blank" >college basketball</a> when everything gets kicked up a notch or five, including fans’ complaints.</p><p>The refs are against us. The gods are against us. Even the announcers are against us.</p><p>And if you’re rooting for Illinois, there’s a pretty good chance the location for its NCAA Tournament <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/22/illinois-ncaa-houston-cougars-illini-sweet-16-march-madness" target="_blank" >Sweet 16 game against Houston</a> (9:05 p.m. Thursday, TBS, truTV) is on your list of beefs before the first dribble. As if the Cougars (30-6), the South Region’s No. 2 seed, wouldn’t be enough of a challenge for the No. 3-seeded Illini (26-8) in any old gym, this one is at the Toyota Center in Houston.</p><p>Whoa, that’s rough. But “woe is me,” it better not be.</p><p>That message is clear from the Illini going in.</p><p>“We play Houston,” center Zvonimir Ivisic said. “We don’t play the fans.”</p><p>“They’re the higher seed,” forward Jake Davis said, “so it is what it is.”</p><p>It’s all about perspective, according to their coach.</p><p>“I’ll be honest, I couldn’t care less,” Brad Underwood said.</p><p>“I’m an old juco ball coach. I drove 16-passenger vans. I drove from Dodge City, Kansas, to Mesa, Arizona, for a basketball game, for a tournament, in a bus. If you had told me back then that I’m getting to coach basketball in the Sweet 16 and play Houston, I would sign up for it. I would crawl to get there.”</p><p>Sweet 16 matchups don’t get much more worth the time than this one.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/22/illinois-ncaa-houston-cougars-illini-sweet-16-march-madness" >Illinois’ NCAA path blocked by Houston, a powerhouse that will be hard to handle in Sweet 16</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/22/big-ten-packed-sweet-16-the-dream-scenario-for-championship-starved-conference" >Is Big Ten-packed Sweet 16 the dream scenario for championship-starved conference?</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/15/my-ncaa-tournament-pick-arizona-wildcats-big-12-final-four-march-madness" target="_blank" >My NCAA Tournament pick: Arizona will win it all in a Big 12-heavy Final Four</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Underwood has been boasting all season about having the best offense in the country, and the metrics have backed up his claim, though Purdue has edged a whisker in front of the Illini into the No. 1 spot in the KenPom efficiency rankings. Houston is fourth in defensive efficiency and second in scoring defense, allowing a mere 62.2 points per game.</p><p>Each team is led in scoring, assists and steals by a freshman point guard bound for the NBA draft lottery. Illinois’ 6-6 Keaton Wagler takes his time creating space for threes and drives and loads up on free throws. Houston’s 6-4 Kingston Flemings is much quicker getting to his spot and expert in the pull-up game.</p><p>Speaking of beefs, Illini fans still have an old one with Cougars coach Kelvin Sampson, infamous for flipping Illini commit Eric Gordon in 2006, when Sampson was at Indiana.</p><p>“I would expect there to be a lot of Illinois fans here,” Sampson said. “They have a tremendous program, terrific fan base.”</p><p>A year ago in Indianapolis, Houston overcame partisan Purdue and Tennessee crowds in the Sweet 16 and Elite Eight, respectively, en route a national-runner-up finish. Sampson’s team knows it can win anywhere, against anybody, period.</p><p>Underwood's ought to be able to say the same by now.</p><p>“If we want to beat them, no matter where we play them, we would have to play great,” Underwood said. “Guess what? We’re going to have to do that [Thursday]. And I think they’re going to have to play well if they want a chance to beat us.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/hmeG3uVuB4s?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Houston pregame NCAA Tournament press conference: Brad Underwood and Illini players"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p></p>
entries.author: Steve Greenberg

entries.title: State lawmakers advance bill to keep immigration detention centers out of communities like suburban Broadview
entries.summary: <p>State lawmakers are taking steps to, in the future, keep immigration detention centers out of communities. </p><p>Under a bill that moved out of an Illinois House committee Wednesday, new immigration detention centers would be prohibited within 1,500 feet of schools, churches, day care centers, cemeteries, public parks, forest preserves, private residences and public housing. </p><p>Testifying in support of the legislation was House Speaker Emanuel “Chris” Welch and Broadview Mayor Katrina Thompson, both of whom represent the Broadview area, where a U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement processing center played a major role in Operation Midway Blitz last fall. The bill got broad support from Democrats, but was unanimously opposed by Republicans.</p><p>“This bill says something very simple and very reasonable: Detention facilities do not belong in the middle of our neighborhoods,” Welch said. “They should not be next to schools, they should not be next to day care centers, they should not sit beside parks, public housing, places of worship, or private homes like the Broadview detention center does.”</p><p>Welch noted that within 1,500 feet of the Broadview facility, there are two day care centers, a park, a place of worship and several private residences. The bill would not close the current Broadview center. </p><p>That facility became a flashpoint for anti-ICE protests during the deportation campaign. Six protesters, nicknamed<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/16/chicago-broadview-six-kat-abughazaleh-midway-blitz-trump-ice" > the Broadview Six, </a>were charged with conspiracy, a federal felony, following their participation in protests at the facility. Two of the six have had charges dropped. </p><p>Thompson testified about the financial strain of housing the Broadview center put on her community, which amounted to more than <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/24/broadview-mayor-demands-closure-of-ice-facility-reimbursement-of-village-costs-business-losses-police-fire-congress-trump-detention" target="_blank" ><u>$700,000</u></a>, according to village officials.</p><p>“For a small community like ours this is not just a number, that is a significant burden on our taxpayers, and yet there has been no reimbursement,” Thompson said.</p><p>Rep. Christopher Davidsmeyer, a Republican from Jacksonville, voted against the legislation, calling it just a "political bill." He expects it to be challenged in the courts if it passes.</p><p>“The reality is our laws have actually been pushing ICE’s actions in the directions they have been going,” Davidsmeyer said. “So if we would just work with them, things could happen peacefully and we wouldn't have this problem.”</p><p>The bill awaits votes in both the House and Senate. Several committee members asked to be added as co-sponsors to the bill. </p>
entries.author: Matt Trunfio

entries.title: What to know about Chicago's delivery robots and the debate over them
entries.summary: <p>When Lincoln Park resident Ainsley Harris first spotted a delivery robot in her neighborhood last year, she said it wasn’t the technology that caught her attention. It was the look.</p><p>The boxy, pink and orange robot felt more playful than mechanical, Harris said. Even “kind of cute.” It was one of a fleet of robots belonging to Coco Robotics, a Los Angeles-based sidewalk delivery company that <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/technology/2025/01/14/coco-delivery-robot-chicago" >began appearing in some Chicago neighborhoods in late 2024.</a></p><p>But things changed for her when robot operations began to ramp up in Lincoln Park, she said. A mother of two, Harris is responsible for a stroller, a labradoodle and a young kid on a bike when she goes out for walks. More robots on her neighborhood's narrow sidewalks mean she has to be even more aware to ensure her family's safety.</p><p>"The more time that I spent with them, and the more close contact I had with them ... the more I began to kind of sour on them and having them in the neighborhood," Harris, 42, said.</p><p>Harris isn't alone in her sentiment. Josh Robertson, one of Harris' neighbors, <a class="Link" href="https://nosidewalkbots.org/" target="_blank" >started a petition</a> last summer to call on the city to pause the sidewalk robot program until it released safety findings on the devices and set rules for how they're deployed. The petition has received over 3,700 signatures from more than 50 ZIP codes across the city.</p><p>"Sidewalks are for people and should remain people first," Robertson, 40, said.</p><p>As companies push to dispatch more of the delivery robots across Chicago neighborhoods, Robertson said he hopes his petition will place pressure on city officials to pause the services. </p><p>Here's an overview of the current state of robot delivery in the city — and how Chicagoans are responding.</p><h3>What companies are behind Chicago’s delivery robots?</h3><p>Coco Robotics and Serve Robotics are the two tech companies that have rolled out delivery robots across the city the last two years.</p><p>Coco's robots began appearing in the 27th and 34th wards as part of an initial pilot program. Serve Robotics, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/business/2025/09/29/look-out-coco-the-food-delivery-robots-introduced-in-late-2024-now-have-competition" target="_blank" >another Los Angeles-based sidewalk robotics company</a>, launched 50 robots across much of the city's North and West sides last September, adding to the growing fleet of robotic delivery couriers.</p><p>The robots were first allowed by the city in 2022, when then-Mayor Lori Lightfoot announced the City Council had approved of a Personal Delivery Device pilot program. This program allowed for local food establishments to partner with the companies to use their small robots to deliver food to customers. Throughout the launch of the program, the Chicago Department of Transportation carried out an assessment of the robots to see if they could be used safely without inconveniencing sidewalk users.</p><p>The program continues and will not renew past May 2027 without action from the City Council, according to the city's website. In December, <a class="Link" href="https://311.chicago.gov/s/article/Personal-Delivery-Devices-PDD?language=en_US" target="_blank" >the city released a 311 category</a> to encourage Chicago residents to submit their concerns and feedback about the robots.</p><h3>Where in the city can you find a delivery robot?</h3><p>There are 100 delivery robots up and running in the city.</p><p>Coco's 50 robots operate in Fulton Market, the West Loop, River West, the Loop, South Loop and Greektown.</p><p>Serve's 50 robots operate in 14 neighborhoods: Austin, Belmont Cragin, Dunning, East Garfield Park, Humboldt Park, Lake View, Lincoln Park, Little Italy, Logan Square, Near North Side, Near West Side, Uptown, West Garfield Park and West Town.</p><p>Neither company's robots operate on the city's South Side.</p><h3>Do they deliver anything other than food?</h3><p>Both Serve and Coco have partnerships with Uber Eats for food and restaurant deliveries.</p><p>Coco robots also supports delivery for certain grocery and everyday essentials through its partnership with DoorDash and DashMart, according to a representative for Coco robots. Serve's director of government affairs, Yariel Diaz, said the company hopes to expand "beyond food delivery to groceries, medications, parcels and more."</p><p>Customers in the neighborhoods where robot delivery is available are able to choose their preferred delivery method when placing an order in their app of choice. Users can also opt out of robot delivery entirely.</p><h3>Do they accept tips?</h3><p>No. Robots don't accept tips.</p><h3>Can the robots deal with snow and ice?</h3><p>Both companies say their robots are equipped to operate in winter conditions, but <a class="Link" href="https://www.youtube.com/shorts/NGIR5it1gSE" target="_blank" >one city resident documented</a> a robot getting stuck in the snow this winter.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-520000" name="image-520000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ee9d680/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4256x2389+0+224/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F22%2F13%2F685ea1e941eb816bb16eb28eb4b8%2Fdh1-2469-2.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2881094/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4256x2389+0+224/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F22%2F13%2F685ea1e941eb816bb16eb28eb4b8%2Fdh1-2469-2.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f8173ec/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4256x2389+0+224/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F22%2F13%2F685ea1e941eb816bb16eb28eb4b8%2Fdh1-2469-2.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="DH1_2469-2.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f8173ec/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4256x2389+0+224/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F22%2F13%2F685ea1e941eb816bb16eb28eb4b8%2Fdh1-2469-2.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3a2df73/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4256x2389+0+224/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F22%2F13%2F685ea1e941eb816bb16eb28eb4b8%2Fdh1-2469-2.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f8173ec/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4256x2389+0+224/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F22%2F13%2F685ea1e941eb816bb16eb28eb4b8%2Fdh1-2469-2.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A Serve Robotics food delivery robot goes on a test run downtown earlier this month.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>How have residents and alderpersons responded to the robots?</h3><p>Several alderpersons across the city have begun surveying their residents to gauge interest in expanding robot services into their neighborhoods.</p><p>Alds. Bennett Lawson (44th) and Angela Clay (46th) sent surveys to their respective constituents in November. Ald. Daniel La Spata (1st) hosted a virtual meeting with representatives from both robot companies and constituents Feb. 2 to give residents in his ward an opportunity to ask questions and share concerns. In addition to the meeting, his offices also collected feedback.</p><p>In a statement released Tuesday, his office said robot deliveries will not be expanding further in the ward after 83.7% of 1st Ward respondents strongly disagreed with the expansion.</p><p>"While I will not be asking Coco to pull back from their limited pilot area east of Wood Street, neither company will be expanding further into the ward," he said in the statement.</p><p>Since starting his petition, Robertson said he's added an optional incident field where people can share their experiences with the robots. He said he has seen around 850 incident reports on his form, as individuals reported incidents of collisions, injuries and obstruction of sidewalks around their neighborhoods.</p><p>"These robots have had a real tangible effect for Chicagoans going about their lives at street and sidewalk levels," Robertson said.</p><h3>What do delivery drivers and couriers think?</h3><p>Efrain Berrocal, a courier and dispatch coordinator at Chicago-based Cut Cats, a courier service run by cyclists, said the robots may pose a threat to traditional courier services.</p><p>"We haven't seen it be an issue so far, but I could see it becoming an issue when, inevitably, restaurants start to use more 3rd party apps, and then those 3rd party apps start to get their own robots," he said. "Then ... we might see an issue."</p><p>To combat this, the 12-year-old business that has worked with over 85 restaurants and businesses in the city, continues to market itself as a local group, where humans are part of the entire process.</p><p>But both Coco and Serve argue the robots are a boon both for businesses and human couriers.</p><p>In a statement, a Coco representative said that Chicago is well suited for short-distance deliveries because of the city's density and walkability.</p><p>"For restaurants and retailers, robots help make last-mile delivery more efficient by reducing congestion, lowering delivery costs and smoothing peak demand, especially during busy meal times," the representative said.</p><p>According to Serve's website, when the robots focus on short-distance delivery, "human couriers are able to focus on higher-value deliveries and restaurants can stay focused on cooking and in-store guests."</p><p>The website also states that servicing the robot operations creates new roles in remote assistance, fleet operations and maintenance. "Robots act as a supplemental tool alongside human workers, not a replacement," the website said.</p><h3>Have any incidents happened with these robots?</h3><p>A CoCo Robotics delivery robot <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/25/delivery-robot-smashes-into-old-town-bus-shelter-in-second-crash-of-the-week" target="_blank" >shattered the glass</a> of an Old Town bus shelter March 24, a day after a video of a Serve Robotics delivery robot rolling through the shattered glass of the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/24/delivery-robot-goes-viral-after-shattering-west-town-bus-shelter-glass" target="_blank" >bus shelter it destroyed in West Town</a> went viral earlier the same week.</p><p>Serve Robotics said it was aware of the incident and worked with the bus shelter provider to clean up the damage. The company was also reviewing what happened in an attempt to make improvements.</p><p>“We have also been in contact with local stakeholders and are committed to addressing any concerns directly,” Serve Robotics said in a statement. “We take this matter very seriously.”</p><p>Carl Hansen, vice president and head of government relations at Coco Robotics, said the robots operate at a top speed of 5 mph and "safety is a top priority," and thus they were also investigating after helping to clean up the bus shelter.</p><p>“Across more than one million miles of deliveries, this is the first time one of our robots has collided with a structure like this," Hansen said in a statement. “While this appears to be a rare, isolated event, we are committed to learning from it and ensuring it does not happen again.”</p><p>Ald. Walter Red Burnett (27th) confirmed Coco Robotics was in touch with JC Decaux — an advertising company, that builds and maintains the shelters through an agreement with the city — to restore the shelter. Burnett noted residents' concerns in the wake of the back to back incidents.</p><p>“We are stressing the importance of public safety and consistent monitoring of these robots to prevent harm to people, infrastructure, and property,” Burnett said in a March 25 statement. “We will consider their performance in complying with safety protocols before making a determination on their presence in the ward following the pilot."</p><h3>Which is faster — a robot or a human?</h3><p>In a statement sent via email, a representative for Coco robots said: "Robot delivery isn't designed to compete on raw speed. The bigger goal is easing congestion in cities by taking short car trips off the road. Coco robots move at pedestrian pace, up to about 5 mph, and intentionally slow down around people or obstacles. The focus is efficiency, not rushing, helping complete short delivery trips in a predictable, low-impact way while reducing traffic and emissions."</p><p>According to Serve robots website, its average delivery time is about 18 minutes.</p>
entries.author: Elleiana Green

entries.title: Months overdue, downtown's State Street Bridge set to reopen Friday
entries.summary: <p>The State Street Bridge is scheduled to reopen on Friday after a nearly year-long closure for emergency repairs, according to the Chicago Department of Transportation.</p><p>First constructed in 1949, it was one of four bridges closed for repairs or replacement in the last year. The closures have caused headaches for commuters, and complaints from some City Council members who have pressured the Transportation Department for greater transparency around planned bridge closures.</p><p>Before the closure, nearly 7,000 vehicles passed over the bridge daily, with roughly one-tenth of them being trucks, according to a 2025 inspection filed with the state.</p><p>City officials expected the State Street Bridge repairs to take seven months <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2025/04/11/state-street-bridge-repair" target="_blank" >when the bridge was suddenly closed</a> to traffic in April 2025 for emergency repairs. But engineers discovered the bridge was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/01/14/state-street-bridge-repairs-expected-to-be-complete-by-end-of-february-open-to-traffic-in-march" target="_blank" >in worse shape than first thought</a>, extending the closure for several months.</p><p>The bridge is one of several downtown bascule bridges which can be raised to allow river traffic to pass underneath. According to CDOT, the repairs included: replacing the bridge's center locks and its center joint, which caused damage to the roadway during periods of extreme heat; realigning the bridge leaves; fixing the State Street viaduct and sidewalks just north of the river; and replacing the bridge's floorbeam and bracing.</p><p>Once the State Street Bridge reopens, the city will have at least three ongoing bridge replacements: the Chicago Avenue Bridge isn't <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2025/08/04/chicago-traffic-construction-halsted-street-avenue-department-transportation-cdot-craig-turner" target="_blank" >scheduled to reopen</a> until December; the <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicago.gov/city/en/depts/cdot/provdrs/bridge/news/2025/september/cortland-street-bridge-rehabilitation-project-to-begin-september.html" target="_blank" >Cortland Street Bridge</a>, the city's oldest bascule bridge, could be closed until September 2027; and the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2025/09/28/lake-street-bridge-repairs-closure-chicago" target="_blank" >Lake Street Bridge isn’t expected to reopen</a> until January 2028.</p><p>The city is <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicago.gov/content/dam/city/depts/cdot/bridge/news/Division%20PIM%20Presentation_2024-0326%20FINAL%20(002).pdf" target="_blank" >also preparing</a> to replace two Division Street bridges over the North Branch of the Chicago River, but the timing of those closures have not yet been announced.</p>
entries.author: David Struett

entries.title: As Ohio State star Kayden McDonald prepares to visit Bears, he says, 'I'm their No. 1 guy'
entries.summary: <p>COLUMBUS, Ohio — Defensive tackle Kayden McDonald never lets up, and as he ripped through tackling dummies and blasted into blocking sleds at Ohio State’s pro day Wednesday, his screams echoed through the fieldhouse as representatives from every NFL team, including a dozen head coaches and general managers, watched nearby.</p><p>McDonald is a true monster in the middle and grew into a first-round prospect for the upcoming draft, where <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >the Bears</a> have their eyes on him at No. 25. He’s everything they’ve been missing since the days of Akiem Hicks.</p><p>“I’m just a powerful player,” McDonald told the Sun-Times. “You know what you’re going to get out of me. I’m the strongest guy on the defense and I’m a natural. Football comes easy for me.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/o5kkzbQEl8Y?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Big Mac 🍔 | Kayden McDonald NFL Draft Report & Scouting Profile"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>He’s widely projected to go late in the first round, and the Bears have shown significant interest. While general manager Ryan Poles was at Alabama pro day on Wednesday, likely with some interest in offensive tackle Kadyn Proctor, the team had a scout tracking McDonald at Ohio State.</p><p>McDonald said his first pre-draft visit will be a trip to Halas Hall on April 1 and has gotten a strong impression that he stands atop the Bears’ draft board if he’s still around at No. 25.</p><p>“They’ve talked to me a lot,” he said. “I’m their No. 1 guy. That’s what I feel like. They seem very excited to get me up there.</p><p>“They say they like how I play and they like my motor. I play all four quarters and I’m relentless.”</p><p>He has some work to do on countermoves in the pass rush and gets overly aggressive at times, perhaps veering from his assignment in order to try for a big play, but there’s little doubt about the impact and presence he brings to the line of scrimmage.</p><p>McDonald got attention as a backup to first-round pick Tyleik Williams during Ohio State’s 2024 national championship season, then carried that momentum into a starting role last season. He had 65 tackles, including nine for loss, forced two fumbles and was voted an All-American.</p><p>He’s one of five Buckeyes likely to go in the first round next month, joining pass rusher Arvell Reese, safety Caleb Downs, linebacker Sonny Styles and wide receiver Carnell Tate. If that happens, the program will have produced 15 first-round picks in last five drafts.</p><p>Playing under defensive coordinator Matt Patricia, the former Lions head coach and Patriots coordinator, McDonald frequently played over the center and constantly was “just knocking people back,” as coach Ryan Day put it. He was a force against the run and as a pass rusher and had three sacks last season.</p><p>The Bears badly need that type of disruption in the middle of their defensive line, which has been a persistent problem for Poles throughout his tenure.</p><p>After pouring major resources into the unit, including a second-round pick to draft Gervon Dexter and a big contract for veteran Grady Jarrett, they allowed the sixth-most yards rushing in the NFL last season. They also were tied for the seventh-fewest sacks.</p><p>The Bears have been bottom-five in the league in run defense and sacks since Poles took over in 2022.</p><p>With Dexter going into the final season of his contract and the Bears holding a manageable out on Jarrett’s deal after this season, reloading on the defensive line would make sense. They drafted defensive lineman <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2025/04/25/texas-a-m-defensive-tackle-shemar-turner-really-checks-the-boxes-for-bears-dc-dennis-allens-defense" >Shemar Turner</a> in the second round last year, and he’s likely to play defensive end once he’s back from a torn ACL.</p><p>It’s nearly impossible to be a strong defense without difference makers up front, and the last two Super Bowl winners, the Eagles and Seahawks, won with waves of talented defensive linemen. The Bears have some catching up to do in that area, and an explosive player like McDonald could move them closer to that level.</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module
